# Draft in
# Growing Product People

When the call came in it was actually a series of texts from one of my oldest friends, whom I’ll call “AJ”. He told me that a privacy-oriented open source project at the MIT media lab was spinning out a volunteer-based nonprofit to build a non-surveilling contact tracing app and could I “help with the product”?

The timing was perfect. I was recently at the end of a four-year stint as VP of product at an extremely lean innovative behavioral health moonshot, and had resumed a consulting practice coaching business leaders, clarifying product roadmaps, sorting out information architectures, recommending baseline product ops cadences and practices, and most often helping leaders identify, recruit, and make their first product hire or hire their first head of product.

Depending on the scale of the company this could be a hands-on PM or an executive. It might be the first and only product hire or there may already be product managers in the company but no leadership.

In a healthy early-stage company one of the founders should be “running product” until the product-market fit is established and an MVP is launched or close.

A product person generally prefers the term “product” to the more cumbersome (and loaded) “product management” and a shibboleth of the product tribe is using the word “product” by itself with very little ceremony. “I’m product at company x.” “I do the product.” “I’m a product person.” I used to call myself “Product guy to the stars” till guy felt too excluding, plus an LA friend asked me which stars and then I had to explain it was just an expression and nevermind….

Many larger companies (and some consulting agencies) are only now grafting product practices onto their software development lifecycles, and may still be showing many of the seams from having recently introduced and adapted to UX, design thinking, and user-centered design.

So back to the coronavirus pandemic response project. My consulting had slowed down a bit as the nation gradually started sheltering in place and decision-makers in general seemed to be taking a “wait and see” attitude to their upcoming spending and hiring plans.

Fortunately, I had just pitched the idea for this book to the Sense & Respond crew and in that sense the timing was perfect. Aside from jail, a lockdown might be the ideal scenario for getting a book written (sure, ok, in an emotionally frictionless universe, otherwise there is some risk of off-the charts isolation and aggravated writer’s cramp).

And then the call, or text rather, from AJ came in and I signed up for the slack and there was already a product in beta, on github, and the core group was working 20 hours a day seven days a week and it felt like the most urgent priority in the world (and since I am neither a first responder nor an essential worker, it basically was the most urgent thing I had encountered since the news of the pandemic spread).

So they were coding a mile a minute, but there was no roadmap, there was no UX strategy, there were a number of assumptions about the “customer” (regular people who may or may not be infected with a new virus) that did not appear to have been de-risked yet, and there were a handful of product managers and people I’d call “product curious” milling around in the slack, getting no traction, unable to provide value to the software developers, and either losing interest or finding a role on an adjacent team, such as comms or program management.

Not only did this offer me something, anything I could do to feel as though I had some agency (beyond streaming TV and chilling) but it was also almost the ideal laboratory for growing product people under incredible pressure with very high stakes.

Put me in, coach

Yes, I said to AJ. Of course I’ll help any way I can. I’ll help with that roadmap. I’ll help with the product. Just point me to it.

This did mean, to the frustration of my gracious publishers and to some extent myself as well, that my schedule for writing this book ended up slipping by about six months!

So job one for me now was to round up the product people, new and experienced. Gather them into a scrum group, identify priorities, and start providing value to the rest of the org.

We were also going to have to get everyone to agree on what we mean when we say “product,” get buy-in that the technology part of the project needed to be managed with a “product mindset,” distinguish product discovery from product management from product ownership (not to mention project management), and clarify exactly how product people could provide both immediate tactical value and long-term strategic focus to the project.

Along the way, I was going to have to identify product people with varying skills sets: product owners with scrum master experience, release managers, product discovery and ux research principals, feature- and theme-directing product managers, product team leadership that could make the case for product thinking at the executive level.

Which raises the question, What exactly makes someone a “product person”? I’ll give you a way to answer that question for yourself in the next chapter but before we rush right past the elephant in the room, what exactly are we talking about when we say “product”?

What exactly is a (digital software) “product”?

So let’s get a couple of terms and concepts defined for the sake of this book.

In software terms, a product is a package of features and services delivered to a market to address a hypothetical need or “job”. Digital software product management is a relatively young discipline with roots in 20th century industrial product development, software program management, business analysis, Silicon Valley-style technical product management, user-centered design, and lean startup practices.

A product manager is responsible for defining and delivering a fix, feature, product, or product line, depending on the scope of their responsibility.

This is what products used to look like.

A product owner works closely on or with an engineering team to groom a backlog of potential features and fixes, plan sprints, manage scrums, run retrospectives, etc. — may or may not also be a scrum master, engineering manager / team lead, or (usually relatively junior, tactical) product manager.

Product management is not the same thing as project management, although most product managers, especially product owners, have some project management among their obligations. At more senior levels this more often manifests as program management and product operations or “product ops” (the structure, organization, and cadences of the product development process).

Key takeaways
- A company’s first product hire (or first head of product) will have a huge impact on the direction of the product.
- It’s key to establish a shared understanding of the meaning and value of both product management as a practice and of fostering a product mindset across your entire business.
- In addition to hiring experienced product professionals, success will also depend on identifying and cultivating the instincts of potential product people already on staff in many different roles concerned with the making of valuable software experiences.

# 6 min read
# Draft in
# Growing Product People

The diagram. Every discipline has one. It’s often a venn diagram but not always. It divides work into categories or practices and overlaps them. Generally, the profession in question defines itself as the linchpin for these competing concerns that have to find some harmonious way to coexist.

These diagrams aren’t so much wrong as they are subjective. As long as you are aware that the adjacent disciplines probably have their own diagram that puts them at the center of the universe and you don’t make them signing off on your worldview a prerequisite for getting along, you can describe your work in any way that helps your community of practice work through it together.

So you won’t be surprised to learn that product management has its own canonical diagram, made famous by Mind the Product founder Martin Eriksson:

Lo and behold, product management finds itself in the navel of the universe.

Again it’s not wrong, but I could swear that when I was a UX director I felt like we were the ones holding it all together, connecting what the customers were trying to tell us with what the business needed and the technology dictates through the magic of user-centered design.

Fortunately product managers are generally pretty good at “taking the piss” out of themselves, as an occupational necessity, so the above image has inspired a generation of riffs and memes, with this variant being my personal favorite:

I am reliably informed that as long as “bring the donuts” you won’t actually be hated by everyone.

Let’s use this diagram instead

But there is a diagram I like a lot better these days for helping people understand what product people do, and it gets away from this simple mechanistic notion of balancing or stitching together a handful of discrete adjacent disciplines.

Joff Redfern, a VP of Product at Atlassian calls this the “PM Craft Triangle”:

Joff Redfern’s PM Craft Triangle

At the apex, the triangle is labeled General Manager (GM), representing the business leadership and ownership of the bottom line that represents a major aspect of product craft. (A lot of people call a product manager the “CEO of the product.” They’re wrong but we’ll come back to that.)

A product manager is not the CEO of the product.

At the left hand is the Artist. As you can see these are not jobs or subdisciplines of the software development lifecycle but temperaments, aptitudes, cultivated sensibilities. The artist is creative, sees things in fresh ways, makes beautiful things that engage the senses. You know the type.

And in the right corner, we have the Scientist, the seeker of evidence, the formulator of hypotheses, the measurer, the experimenter, that statistician.

In Joff’s telling (I’ll ask him to take a look at this before publication), most of us start off in one of those corners, based on our own primary or innate tendencies. Cultivating product craft in ourself or others involves movement toward the center, learning about and growing by incorporating the wisdom of the other pillar temperaments.

(This movement often starts by progressing along an edge toward one of the other two point of view.)

Highly skilled product professionals make it to smaller triangle that more fully incorporates these three cross-pressures:

The smaller triangle represents more mature craft, no longer hugging the corner of one or the edge of just two of the three points of view

Even once you (or your team) have “made it” to the inner triangle, it’s quite natural that each product person will still hang out closer to the corner they started in:


When I was five years old I told people I wanted to be an artist “and a lawyer” — maybe I was ahead of my time.

The fourth essential temperament

Going back to original craft pyramid, I’d personally add one more temperament that I have found shared by by most of the best product people I have worked with:


Yes, the supreme talent of the best product people is nosiness. If you aren’t willing to stick your nose into everybody’s business, this may not be your bag.

This temperament is that of the Nosy Person, or “nosiness.” You’ve got to be a busybody. 

Product people are the ones who look at the leadership team meeting in a room all day arguing over company strategy and roadmap and think “I want to be in that room. They need me in that room.”

A product person has to be a jack of all trades, fluent in the argot of each of their colleagues’ tribes, intensely curious about the what and the how and the why of every aspect of putting the product together and shipping it into the hands and minds of real people.

And you need to be able to do this without freaking people out!

You have to quietly know the whole plan and who is doing which part of it and how well that part is going and you’re going to be the one who raises the alarm when the date slips or the traffic spikes or the reviews are going south on the app store, and you’re going to be the one waking up in the middle of the night to remember that there is one more language still incomplete for the internationalization and localization deadline.

Nosiness will take you far.

Meanwhile, back in Cambridge

Before plowing ahead, let’s check in on the project initially called Private Kit and later Safe Paths and eventual PathCheck. A lot of people were saying the product needed focus and the product needed help, and a lot of people were showing up and saying they could fix the product or that they had been recruited to run the product or be head of product.

Many of them were correct, too, as people in the founding leadership repeatedly reached out to experts in their network but without a mature vocabulary for distinguishing a tech business from an engineering team from a product organization.

Leadership was crying out for an “MVP spec” they could use to show to partners, potential investors, and so on. Serious product folks with legitimate principles pushed back against this top-down approach, possibly misreading the pace of the moment and the limited value of “best practices” when chasing a pandemic at breakneck speed.

I agreed to pull together that doc and started identifying the user stories already defined and mapped by the UX volunteers to figure out which were in scope for MVP and to get buy-in from all the stakeholders.

Meanwhile, after rallying the product-identified people in the project slack, I next met with the engineering leads and asked them the key question: How can I help?

It became clear that there was no testing or quality assurance going on yet and while this is not my forte, I certainly know how to write up a few manual test cases based on user story happy paths and some obvious alternative flows, edge conditions, and error cases, so I set to work doing just that and almost like magic within a few weeks and amazing Testing lead (Diarmid Mackenzie) appeared out of Italy and took the entire volunteer QA program to professional heights in remarkably short order.

When this started happening, I knew it was time to return to the cabal of devs and founders cranking out the cowboy code and ask them the same question as before: What can I help with?

Key takeaways
- Product does indeed sit at the junction of UX/design, technology, and business in all its myriad wonderful manifestations (from marketing to data analysis to customer success to partnerships and more).
- But more importantly, great product people are strongly rooted in one or more of the mindsets of a general manager, artist, or analytical scientist, and professional and career growth for the individual comes from strengthening complementary skills and points of view.
- Likewise a great product team will strive for similar balance and complementarity drawing on all of these critical ways of solving problems and creating things that work.
- The best product people are also profoundly nosy. Go ahead and read them in.

# 8 min read
# Draft in
# Growing Product People

Taking a step back, and looking at the core qualities of a product person reviewed in the previous post, I have to mention a further quality shared by all great product people: 

Obsessive customer focus.

In fact, the folks at Folding Burritos took it upon themselves to recontextualize that canonical venn diagram I shared at the top in a much larger critical context:

shamelessly stolen from foldingburritos.com

The silicon valley school of product management kicked off by author and consultant Marty Cagan and the lean startup movement both share the commitment to “getting outside the building,” which exhorts product people to look up from their graphs and their draft specs and their “jobs to be done” and make sure they are spending part of every week meeting and talking to real people.

UX people are (or should be) your allies in this drive to understand the habits and needs of the people whose problems you are solving.

Customer discovery across the product life cycle

Products have life cycles. They start off as ideas or hypotheses in search of validation. In these earliest stages, you don’t yet know if you have “product-market fit.” You may be going on a hunch or you may be responding to a signal from another product effort (or pivoting from a preceding hypothesis).

Early stage startups, innovation labs, skunkworks, and any generative product team that is empowered to meet their goals creatively may be in the process of finding a product-market fit to build on.

At this stage the assignment is:

-ully understand the needs of a specific type of customer’s job to be done and
invent a sufficiently valuable improvement to how that job is done today to persuade this type of customer to adopt a new habit.

An eye for product-market fit

After we sold a startup to Dropbox I took a few months off to get my “work-life balance” back together, to stop eating garbage, to spend time at home with my family, to play music, and to find a meaningful next product to sink my teeth into.

In due time I discovered a recently graduated Ycombinator startup that was looking for someone “to help with their product” or possibly with their UX, or both, it wasn’t 100% clear?

I took a look at their website and did a little bit of digging and was immediately impressed by two things:

1. The website was a confusing hot mess.
2. The service being provided was striking a nerve and drawing in a growing base of users who were somehow managing to find their way through a maze of options to the core experience of a caring, compassionate conversation with a stranger.

This company (7 Cups) passed my “crawl through glass” test. It’s as if a shopkeeper had literally strewn broken glass on a soggy off-red carpet and nonetheless customers were crawling through that glass to get what was on offer behind the counter.

That’s product-market fit.

I took the job. Optimizing an onboarding flow I can do any day of the week. Striking gold? That’s more of a twice a year on a blue moon kind of a thing.

The product-market fit engine

Right now you can’t go wrong working from Rahul Vohra’s Product-Market Fit Engine. 

Vohra is the founder of Superhuman, a startup that makes an invitation only, fee-based email client for busy executives. The product has been praised for its next-level user experience (comparable, some say, to the feeling of seeing gmail for the first time) and criticized for a confirmation and tracking statistical view for email senders that borders on spooky.

Vohra worked from a widely accepted (because it’s just so good) observation by Sean Ellis, a forerunner of many later growth hackers, who noted that if you ask your customers how they would feel if they could no longer use your product, the critical group to watch is the percentage who respond “very disappointed.”

The unscientific consensus is that if you reach 40% or above you likely have a good product-market fit. Vohra’s engine uses an extended survey to help you segment your audience and then focus on the needs of that “[would be] very disappointed” group of users (while immediately boosting your relative percentage by dropping the least disappointed personas from your roadmap).

The benefit of this process, now available as a coda document, whether you follow it strictly or not, is that it gives a direct operational way to measure and manage to product-market fit until you have achieved it.

Mention it to your product folks to show you know the score.

Actually having customers

If you already have product-market fit, good on you, mate! That means you’ve got a set of early adopters. Nurture them! They are your allies in the evolution of your idea. Learn how to include them in the conversation and address their contributions without losing control of design process.

Allow them to help you but don’t become hostages to your earliest, most “old skool” users either, or you risk never finding that next layer of the onion who need a bit more help discovering the value of your offering (or for whom you need to evolve your offering to make it truly valuable for them as well.

So now, job one is talking to different segments of your own customer base, and you’d be surprised how many businesses actively prevent their software teams from doing this at all!

The next level is talking to people who might represent your next, larger wave of customers.

UX researchers, behaviorial psychologists, scientific marketers, and data scientists can all be great allies in identifying and reaching these next-tier-out potential customer personas.

Making collaborators of your engaged customers

When you have engaged customers first be grateful for this. Gratitude is strongly associated with happiness! Next, do what you can to provide support for your earliest users. If you can’t host a community for all of them, find a subset who can make themselves available as a panel for feedback and discussion and to get a preview for how your new ideas might land with some of your more vocal existing customers.

Don’t just pay lip service to wanting to hear their ideas. It’s not a matter of letting your most committed customers define your roadmap priorities, but a combination of watching the real user behavior visible in your data (and other observations) and listening to what is being asked for, what complaints keep arising, and what is being loved enables you to meaningfully co-evolve your product into something you may never have envisioned at the outset.

Design centered on users

Beyond literal collaboration and co-creation, product management is lost without a close partnership with UX design leaders and practitioners. Product people need to have a deep and thorough understanding of the meaning and value of user-centered design.

I sometimes envision it this way

A product driven business drives a lot of strategy through its product roadmap, and a product is only as good as the experience it delivers to the end customer (or user, it’s ok to say user, really), and the way to deliver a great user experience to your customers is to deeply understand their needs via UX research and user-centered design. Boom.
In a product-centered business, product strategy depends on successful execution of a product strategy (as tracked in a product roadmap), and no product succeeds without providing a valuable experience for its core customers. And the way to understanding customer needs and crafting solutions to meet them is through design, as we’ll get into further in the next chapter.

Dialing in all the signals

Customer obsession means hungrily consuming every possible signal indicating how people are behaving in regard to your product. You have to find ways to measure how they discover your product and become aware of it. What leads them to visit your site or download your app. How many people who start the signup process complete it? Once people have begun using your product which features do they gravitate toward, which do they shun, and which do they try and then give up on?

What are people saying about your product on social media?

What do the reviews look like?

The techniques for understanding your customers (both current and potential) are myriad, and we’ll cover some more specifics throughout the rest of the book. Just be aware that the best picture leverages both quantitative data (for the what) and qualitative research (for the why). In the end, the more signals you can aggregate the clearer a picture you’ll be able to dial in, and then over time when you start to get a feel for your signals and how they model the real world, you can become very sensitive to the emerging patterns and early indicators of opportunities or problems.

Net Promoter Score (NPS) is the devil. There’s nothing wrong with asking people if they would recommend your product, and it’s a very good idea to ask them why, but the weird pseudo scientific NPS scoring system is wack.

Meanwhile, back at the pandemic…

One of the key markers I looked for that told me if a volunteer on the privacy-preserving contact-tracing project was (or had the potential to be) a good product person was if they asked a variation on this question:

“Do we even know if this is what people want?”

To take a step back, the larger organization was working on promulgating privacy principles and encouraging major players (governments, tech giants, health systems) to sign on to these ideas an approaches, preserving anonymity and avoiding inadvertently fostering a surveillance state.

This wasn’t simply a matter of idealism, although concern for privacy was naturally a rallying factor for people drawn to the organization’s mission. There was also a pragmatic hypothesis that if people in various societies (especially those with more individualistic and libertarian traditions) did not believe their location and other personal data would be kept private they would refuse to comply with the use of contact-tracing software, leave their devices at home, or start carrying them around inside tinfoil pouches.

But there was also an embedded assumption the product people noticed: That digital contact tracing was (at least part of) the solution to help meet public health needs addressing a runaway pandemic.

There was logic here. Manual contact tracing relies more on memory and an interview process. Getting permission to upload anonymized location or contact / exposure data could surely speed up this process and help reduce some errors?

But did we know that for sure? This was the question that I looked for when onboarding a volunteer onto the new product team. I had asked it this way myself: “Are we sure they don’t need pencil sharpeners and more clipboards?”

To be fair, there had been some initial validation of the founding hypothesis for the contact tracing software platform we were building, but it was undocumented and rarely articulated, which meant nobody with a product sensibility had yet captured it and shared it with everyone concerned.

This also represented an opportunity.

Key takeaways
- Product people talk to customers on a weekly basis.
- People will crawl through glass for a valuable product.
- Your users are among your best partners in evolving your product.
- Form as rich a picture of your customers and their behavior as you possibly can.
NPS is the devil.
- De-risk your critical assumptions about customers.

docs.filename: Putting the Design in the Product
docs.content:
# 10 min read
# Draft in
# Growing Product People

Confession: I was once a UX designer who went over to “the dark side” (why do I feel like a Sith revealing his Jedi training?). This is both a superpower for me and a blurred line that many leaders already find confusing. Where does product end and UX begin, or vice versa?

Having been making internet things for a few decades now I’ve been given a lot of titles along the way: content strategist, information architect, strategist, interaction designer, UX designer, design manager, product director, and even technical program manager.

What hasn’t changed is that I’ve always been deeply involved in figuring out what to build, why, and how. I’ve also witnessed the rise (and sometimes fall) of a parade of roles, disciplines, and identities all contributing to our understanding of how to make software for an internetworked world.

It never hurts to bear in mind that the rise of UX, interaction design, design thinking, lean startup, jobs to be done, and (dare I say it) product people — just to choose a few at random — may also serve broader agendas beyond professional development and business theory, providing vehicles for consultants, individual practitioners, “thought leaders,” conferences, schools, certification programs, and so on.

That is UI. This is UX.
There’s a type of meme that just thrives on LinkedIn that compares two objects or two still lives side by side and labels one “UI” and the other “UX.”

Sometimes they are helpful. Usually not.


I’d love to get permission from Jose’ Torre, from his UI vs UX — is Not a Thing. If we can’t get it I will make up a facetious example of our own!
As with so many things, there are four key insights about UI vs. UX:

The term UX gained currency to make a distinction vs. UI. It says “we don’t just design the interface — that’s part of it but we are responsible for before, during after, what, how, and why as well.”

In the realm of competing mindshare, the UX frame pushed traditional communications/graphics designers to the margins, in the UX-savvy “visual designer” subrole, or as illustrators or marketing / communications designers.

Be aware of the technical distinction between the interaction on a single screen and the user journey but pick your battles when it comes to debating the terminology.

Resist the urge to divide your design roles into crude “UI” and “UX” buckets. It’s OK to have designers who are more focused on crafting the interface and others who are more engaged in strategy, systems designs, multi-screen flows, and so on, but it’s all UX so this terminology is misleading and ultimately makes it hard to hire for and build a balanced team.

About “UI/UX”
Inevitably people who were both UX-aware and rooted in traditional design training and education would gravitate toward a role description that also suits the needs of many companies looking to meet all the user experience design needs with a single all-purpose, frictionless task-switching, 24/7 unicorn, “UI/UX designer.”

Please don’t say “UIUX.” It sets my teeth on edge.

Per insight 3 I don’t “correct” people who frame the work as “UI/UX” but I do tend to infer that these nuances might be lost on them, at least at this stage of things, and ultimately I am a descriptivist when it comes to language. It is what it is.

You do need at least one information architect
A somewhat overlooked discipline from the dawn of the web, information architecture (IA), provides a critical missing layer in a lot of software planning and development, what I call the “meaning layer.”

In any complex system there is a picture of what you’re building needed that is not simply a catalog of screens and interactive states, nor is it a sitemap or flow diagram, nor is it a technical stack or a database schema. It sits amidst all of these artifacts and provides a coherent story and picture that explains what we are building for whom, why, and how.


One of a number of great examples Christina Wodtke included in her Five Models for Making Sense of Complex Systems.

This “meaning mapping” exercise can include up to everyone in the org, but is best facilitated and documented by an adept information architect. I recommend that all product managers study IA and learn to use the methods and skills of that practice to provide these maps of meaning that help guide successful projects.

“UX design” or “product design”?
I wouldn’t get hung up on titles, but there is a slight trend in some fields to frame the software design role as product design instead of UX design. This is never intended as an erasure of UX as far as I’ve seen, but it does somewhat take the need for user-centered design as baked in to the whole process already and so maybe less useful as a role distinction.

I’ve seen product designers used in two primary senses:

A “unicorn” (aka the Facebook model). A designers who might be able to or even have some time to do research and strategy who is also a talented interaction designer and visual designer and has time to do both of those jobs and a good prototyper and skilled at writing (at least) front-end code, and good at usability testing, and probably makes their own bitters for their bespoke cocktails, with or without alcohol.
A user-centered designer who is holistically responsible for designing a software product as opposed to an industrial object or an amusement park ride or a billboard, has a product sensibility, thrives in a product org.
It’s probably obvious I prefer the latter connotation but just make sure everyone’s on the same page if you start using that sort of terminology.

Regardless of how you label the job reqs, design is an invaluable problem solving tradition with much to offer to the process of software development, and a laser-like focus on the UX provided across the board by your service is, at end, everybody’s business.

How UX and Product can play well together
UX and Product should be “superfriends” — allies with a shared understanding of the value of designing experiences to meet well understood customer needs. Product managers should refrain from dictating design solutions and designers should respect and be curious about the business and technical constraints shaping the eventual solution.

UX practitioners are given a mandate to be idealistic about the user’s need and concerns. I call them the “tribunes of the people.” There is a common tendency among UX designers (and a lot of great software developers) to want to keep noodling, er sorry, “iterating,” on an idea in search of perfection, at the risk of diminishing return.

Product managers (and, honestly, the best product designers) recognize the need for a bias to action, to push forward with sufficient consensus or a compelling hypothesis and start giving reality a vote in how the product evolves.

Product and UX also need to partner very tightly on product discovery: the stage of understanding the problem space and the day-to-day habits and needs of the target customers. Product can’t just “leave it to the UX researchers” because product isn’t all grooming backlogs, closing sprints, and shipping code. To work together, product managers and UX strategists need to work through differences in their lingo, their point of view, and the burning questions they need to answer, and develop plans to share the time and attention of existing and future customers to make the most of it.

Without tight alignment on the discovery process, there is a huge risk of wasted parallel effort.

Should UX report to Product?
Like so many UX-related questions, the answer to this one is: It depends. The answer tends to have more to do with the skillsets and experience of the leadership ranks of the company, as well as how far into design-lead strategy the company is willing to lean.

A good product lead should be comfortable managing the UX team or working collegially with a UX lead peer.

And, regardless, UX design savvy is table stakes for all product pros.

The flywheel
Most great products have a core value that most users are drawn to and that most new users experience as part of their exploration of the product (the so-called “first-time user experience”).

The design of this first-time experience as a whole, along with features that may appear in an ongoing way all the way through to a user’s full “initiation” into the product’s capabilities, is generally referred to as “onboarding.”

Until you’re sure you’ve nailed that core initial experience, focus on getting that right. Better to have one “A+ good” feature and everything else crappy than to ship an MVP with six C- features and two B+’s. Then, once you’ve achieved fit with that core value proposition, use design methods to craft a series of recurring experiences, including reminders and prompts that fit into the journey of your customer’s existing habits, and that building a whole system and ultimately an engine around that core “flywheel” experience.

Everything else should lead back to giving customers reasons for returning to and repeating that foundational experience, or enhancing it, building off of it, and leveling it up.

Startups do it backward
One thing you may notice if you work in either startups or an entrepreneurial unit in a larger org is that bootstrapping something new sometimes means throwing the book out the window and doing whatever it takes to get from zero to something.

For example, nonprofit project I joined as the pandemic started hitting was in a “cowboy coding” phase of trying to get a minimal set of features working, even if rough around the edges. I often refer to this level of functionality as “the plumbing.”

Getting the plumbing right is necessary but not sufficient for an MVP.

So at this point the team was laser-focused on shipping code. As you might imagine, the earliest software builds had some degree of functionality but very unappealing and hard to understand screens with undifferentiated buttons large all caps block labels and strangely distributed screen real estate.

It wasn’t hard for some talented interaction designers to see many way to make these screens better, so now the project had code with screen designers chasing after the engineers giving them Figma mocks to adapt. So then along came more people skilled in user interface design, interaction design, and user research.


The above screen was a first-draft design for the Safe Places toolkit for mapping and redacting GPS locations to assist with contact tracing. The below screen represents the result of several rounds of design iteration.
Mostly the team just wanted what they called “screen testing” (QA, usability, and user-acceptance testing) of the screens in each build. This was another necessary, but not ultimately sufficient step. Like proofreading, usability testing is a great way to catch mistakes and fix them at the end of the process, but not helpful for anticipating and avoiding errors or for coming up with better ideas in general.

So when I arrived the people to do “real UX research” were getting in line behind some dedicated and persistent volunteers who had gradually introduced more coherent interaction design and some appropriate design ops practices (as well as team management, and copy when needed — thanks for al that, Ali!).

Product folks were at the very end of that line. Now of course, in the long run this is backwards. The “right way” to do all this is to start with product discover and UX research, do interaction design and iteration, testing, etc, and then groom user or job stories with engineer, build, test, and ultimately ship the features.

In reality, each of these practices that naturally “wanted” to precede the others was playing catch-up. Product managers were writing specs to capture what had been built instead of the other way around. UX researchers were studying and de-risking assumptions that were already baked into the technical architecture and the codebase.

Fortunately, at this point a strong product executive talent with deep UX and discovery experience, as well as passion for front-loading product “the right way” showed up on the scene, led the research project to understand how ordinary people felt about contact-tracing, privacy, trust, government, healthcare, surveillance, and so on.


It didn’t say confidential anywhere and plus it’s an open-source project so sharing this screen out of contexts from a research deck should be cool, right?
Sharing and presenting these findings to the working team and the steering leadership made a incredibly strong case for the value and necessity of this kind of work, and so this exec was recruited to take on leadership of the product team I had been kindling and to our great relief she said yes.

Now we were cooking with gas.

Key takeaways
UX is a core product competency. Shipping a great product means designing (and developing and delivering) software that provides a great experience for customers
Information architecture provides a set of essential tool for mapping the meaning layer of a product.
User-centered design, UX design, and design in general provide a set of frameworks, viewpoints, and problem-solving tools well suited to software projects, but ultimately ensuring the delivery of a great user experience is everybody’s business, not just that of the designers.
Whichever UX design specialties your designers excel in (research, interaction design, sketching and studio critique, prototyping, motion design, etc.) consider unifying them under a single banner and mission by dubbing them all product designers.
UX and Product share a broad overlapping set of concerns and work best together when clear boundaries are negotiated when it comes to who makes which decisions.
All great products provide a core flywheel experience that satisfies first-time users and forms the foundation of a potential ongoing user journey that returns to this flywheel and related experiences on the regular.

# 13 min read
# Draft in
# Growing Product People

Code is the material, the substance, that software is made of. It’s where the rubber meets the road. Everything else is just a picture or a plan of what actually gets built.

Whether trained as engineers or not, great product people have a deep understanding of this material: its texture, its grain, where it’s resilient, where it’s brittle, what it’s good for, what it has to be tortured to achieve.

How technical?
At one point I noticed that that friend I’m calling AJ would refer to himself by saying “I’m not technical, but…” I asked him if he would welcome some feedback and when he agreed, I told him that he is technical and he is selling himself short. I pointed out various ways in which he was technically savvy and I said maybe you’re not a technologist (someone who specializes in working with technology professionally) but you’re darn technical. Later he told me another person had offered him the best way to draw a useful line for others, so now he says “I’m not a coder, but….”

A lot of great product managers start off as engineers. It’s not the only path, of course, as we’ve seen, but especially on the silicon valley / lean paradigm engineers and eng managers have always comprised a large chunk of the product manager corps, and to this day often have roles carved out for them with variations on titles such as “technical product manager.”

The word “eng” is pronounced enj.

But even if a product manager might have never themselves worked as an engineer or wouldn’t be able to consistently deliver product-quality code at a professional level, it behooves them nonetheless to become fluent in mySQL, in git, in the preferred IDE (coding environment) the developers are using, in the toolkit and affordances of tech.

In big shops product manager often can’t commit code changes (even typo fixes) or make pull requests, but all product people long to get their “hands on the metal” and prefer direct access to code and data whenever possible.

The fulcrum of collaboration between product and engineering is around architectural decisions and their consequences, and occasionally analogous choices about algorithmic complexity.

In order to participate meaningfully in these conversations, let alone the decisions, the product manager needs to fully fluent in the project’s technical architecture (domain models, layers in the technical stack, the languages, libraries and data structures, message routing, and so on).

Fluency with the technical stack they work on and a deep understanding of its strengths and weaknesses are table stakes for a product person.

Data structures will bite you later if you’re not careful. Decisions you make now will have long term ramifications and you may find engineers pushing back in the future when you want something that today’s architecture didn’t contemplate.

Again, skills in modeling, mapping, graphing, and visualizing complexity come in real handy here in getting everyone on the same page.

The statistician
One of the biggest things that changed for me when I transitioned from UX to Product roles was that I spent a lot less time drawing pictures and a lot more time writing words and looking at numbers.

So. Many. Numbers.

You really can’t do a good job as a product person unless you can be happy spending an entire eight hour day slicing and turning and visualizing tables of numbers that contain all the hidden secrets you are dying to know.

Are signups increasing?

Is churn coming down?

What is the life-time value of our average paying customer? What is the cost of acquisition for a new customer. How is the ratio between those two numbers doing.

Some thing doesn’t feel right about this morning’s north-star metrics. Did we just get mentioned by an influencer? and so on…

Teasing out insights from data. Working with engineers to instrument every critical step along the way of your critical funnels (user flow sequences). Gathering data from wherever you can, allying with researchers and scientists, consuming public and private data sets and slamming them against the data “exhaust” your product has been storing in a “data lake.” Understanding effects of scaling the user base orders of magnitude and the network effects of viral content fanning out across a follower base. These are the daily bread of product management.

Experiments (the mad scientist)
Remember that triangle with the word “scientist” over one one end? Scientist are not engineers (although both are “technical”).

The engineer designs and builds things that work.

Scientists formulate hypotheses and devise experiments to test them, refine models, and gradually accumulate insights into their fields of study.

This restless desire to experiment and discover hitherto unpossessed knowledge enables your team to unleash the potential of a product and unlock all the value it might possess.


A pipeline tool for prioritizing tracking growth experiments (in AirTable) developed by Jesse Avshalomov, co-founder and chief marketing officer at Medinas Health (a pre-owned medical equipment marketplace), who taught me almost everything I know about growth experimentation.
The number one purpose of experiments (this is true for research too) is to hedge your riskiest assumptions. This is often referred to as “de-risking.” The basic point is you can’t test everything and most of the time you don’t need to. Running experiments is as expensive as any other use of planning and development time and time is finite. The most important experiments are the ones that help you mitigate the risks that threaten your fundamental value proposition, and by extension your entire business strategy.

Let me make two more quick points about experiments:

A/B tests (also known as split test, bucket tests, and multivariate tests) are just one of many types of experiments available to you. They can be an excellent way of optimizing a particular experience but they can also result in “polishing a local maximum” at the expense of much more valuable overlooked experiments, and they can function as blinders that prevent you from questioning much more fundamental assumptions about your product experience and what
The product folks running the experiments need to have a thorough understanding of probability, uncertainty, significance. Tests can’t be stopped just because the results are starting to look good, or allowed to run past the target sample size in hopes that they will resolve to a better result. Significance can easily be over-interpreted, leading to business bets being made on the back of random statistical noise or internet “weather.”
At my last startup, I once ran an A/A test. Our hypothesis was that bucket sizes of 2000 users each were enough to yield statistically significant insights. To test this we split all new users into two buckets but then offered them each exactly the same flow. We then ran the experiment until we had 2000 in each bucket. Sure enough, by then the variations had smoothed out and the results in each A bucket were almost identical, and this had not been the case when there were still, say, only 1400 in each bucket (at that point the second A was appearing to beat the first one, for no meaningful reason).

Machine learning
Another aspect of technical acumen that stands product people in good stead these days is at least a passing familiarity with core concepts of data science and particularly machine learning.

Machine learning is a school of AI (artificial intelligence) research in which large data sets are used to “train” algorithms to solve problems or identify results without directly calculating but rather by pattern recognition (this is a very sloppy definition!).

While experiencing a bit of a faddish spike in interest over the past few years (tangled up with chat bots, conversational design, and natural language processing or NLP), the huge potential remains for building products that make use of the data they accumulate to make the services they provide more effective and — for the end user — more magical.

It’s hard enough to build software that runs, let alone software that learns and gets better over time, but even when getting the basics right it’s possible to establish foundations based on understanding those risky assumptions, hypotheses, and experiments that will need to be explored to achieve the desired product experience. Designing with that goal of what might be called the software equivalent of self-knowledge and the ability for self-improvement lays the groundwork for that evolution.

Or as Tom Kerwin put it “A self-improving core feedback loop. It gives feedback to the user, and feedback to itself. And maybe feedback to the builders for how to improve the feedback mechanisms.”

Risk management
As products scale up and user bases grow, problems multiple. Successful populated software attracts criminals, vandals, bored script kiddies, and griefers. Robust platforms promise almost 100% uptime to paying customers. Data must be kept secure, encrypted, and safe from attack. Governments may require that users be given the ability to remove or delete their data.

Performance, reliability, safety, security. These are all entire specialties in software engineering and product managers can make careers focusing on any single one of these factors as well, but most product folks — at least past the proof-of-concept startup phase where scale issues can be safely ignored — spend at least some of their time working through challenges in these dimensions.

Persuasion
All those technical chops, all that STEM education, all that time reading xkcd will pay off in the end if it helps build rapport with engineering teams, show respect for their folkways and the things that inspire and scare them, and build bridges for productive communication and the ability to reach alignment (or, at worst, figure out who isn’t prepared to help you meet your goals).

Speaking geek, knowing the tribal inside jokes, the retro l33t dorkiness, all of these things are a way of showing respect for your colleagues. In my Yahoo days I loved checking in on an internal mailing list called “devel-random”.

Software developers had lists to discuss all the active languages as well as other common reference points (devel-javascript, say, or devel-php), so devel-random was like a water cooler. Hanging out there gave me more insight into the morale and ethos of our engineers than attending 100s of hours of kickoffs and all-hands would ever have. It gave me a way in.

I still remember the day someone on the lists suggested, after our third corporate reorganization in as many years, that we really ought to go out and get the .reorg domain, lmfao.

Back in the dawn of the web, a fellow at MIT named Jeff Bigler articulated the concept of a tact filter, noting a cultural distinction between how self-identified geeks apply a filter for tact “on the way in” (they filter what they hear other people say to them) and thus do not filter what they say to each other for tact “on the way out.” By contrast, “normal” people (in this context, non-engineers) learn to apply their tact filter to what they say and can be put off by the blunt needling style more common in geek culture. (By contrast, geeks can find the euphemisms and circumlocutions of polite speech to be phony and wasteful.) As long as you’re aware of what sort of tact filter the person you’re talking to uses, you can avoid giving or taking needless offense.

Most of the time engineers do not report to product managers. Product people succeed through persuasion, by understanding the needs and motivations (and fears) of the people they’re collaborating with, developing a shared understanding (and, yes, a diagram) of what everyone agrees is the goal, and then making sure everyone feels they have ownership of and a stake in what comes out.

You earn and maintain respect by being reasonable and savvy. The quickest way to lose respect is to “ask for a pony,” to treat the technology like undifferentiated magic and reveal your ignorance of the underlying mechanisms. (By the way, it’s always better to call out your own ignorance and make it clear you are eager to learn rather than attempt to hide what you don’t know.)

Once a product person has earned the respect of their eng peers, this also gives them the ability to “call bullshit” in the friendliest possible way when the dev is playing at “Scotty in the engine room” and padding estimates or shading the truth to avoid acknowledging that an idea is feasible and possibly even good.)


Yoinked from “Can’nae take any more, Captain!” — How operations guys are like Scotty.
Scrum mastery
A huge part of the common ground of product and engineering collaboration takes place these days via agile scrum development practices. Beyond the specifics of rituals such as retrospectives and product demos, the essence of agile (and lean) software development involves decomposing the work into valuable chunks that can be achieved in a limited timeframe (generally a week or two) referred to as a sprint.


A very concave “burndown chart” for a sprint, from The Burndown Chart Reveal Many Secrets by Ziryan Salayi. This one is called “Hurry Up” — see his article for illustrations of “Super Mario,” “Big Finish,” and “Nearly Perfect Burndown.” Personally, I like to bake in an early win up front so you get under that line right away, maaaaaan.
It’s sort of a fractal principal, as it can be applied to a single day or an entire quarter as well, but the general pattern is the same. Define an outcome that is hypothetically achievable in a clear timeframe, make corrections along the way if necessary, and at the end of that time report on what was achieved. Was the goal met? Did it run into problems or change? Was it scoped incorrectly? The point is not perfection but to learn from each iteration and do better on each subsequent cycle of the “cadence.”

Product managers, especially the ones working close to the product owner role, work closely with engineering leads and teams to groom backlogs of wishlist items, prioritizing them against roadmap goals and making difficult tradeoffs. They agree on how much time to spend building things and how much time to spend fixing things, and what alternating rhythm produces the best outcome.

The product manager needs to take the lead in coming up with a “definition of done.” Software projects can easily spin out into fussiness, yak shaving, rabbitholes, and diminishing returns. Product is the home of “good enough.” Other stakeholders need to have a say in this, but ultimately it’s up to product to make the call.

Perfection is a process, not a static state of achievement. There will always be unfixed bugs. If you’re not embarrassed by something about your MVP you waited too long to ship it. Someone has to take ownership of the downside risk of an incorrect decision while ensuring that all the hard work of team results in actually shipping something that actually runs. That’s product.

Product managers have a saying “Good enough is good enough.”

Should Product report to Eng?
A question I get asked a lot when wearing my “product ops” consulting hat is how product and engineering should interact. There are a lot of enterpriwes where these two practices do not get along well. At Yahoo, product and eng were peer orgs and rivals in a long-standing power struggle.

In less technical companies, it may make sense to have an engineering leader in charge of the tech org (say, a CTO) and to have product and engineering managers all reporting into this org. Of course it depends on the size of the teams, the nature of the work, the experience of the leaders.

For my first two years as VP of Product at 7 Cups I also managed the engineering team. It was a small group and I had sufficient technical architecture and team management experience to handle it, but I was never 100% comfortable with that arrangement (despite it being sort of a Holy Grail scenario for a head of product) because the best eng team is an empowered team that runs its own affairs while respecting and collaborating deeply with a strong partner product group.

Over at the nonprofit responding to the coronavirus pandemic, by this time renamed the PathCheck Foundation, we had managed to empower an amazing product lead to set work rallying the troops I had gathered and drilled, building out the team further, organizing it around user-centered aims and introducing serious discovery processes into the development cycle.

At this point the product team had strong executive leadership and reported directly into the CEO (and no longer in to the CTO, who was now leading an Eng organization instead of a broader Tech org, in the parlance).

My work here was done, I thought.

Except not quite, as now the CTO asked me to hop over to his side of the product-engineering divide and help out with the program management there. The positive energy flowing into the strategy and design end of the product practice had an unintended effect of somewhat de emphasizing the execution (or “product owner”) side of things. There were some rough spots in the handoffs and communication loops between orgs, so we did what we could to build up the capacity on the eng side to fully take advantage of the strengths emanating from product.

As someone who’s been wearing product hats for years now, it was a fascinating glimpse at how things had evolved from the most adjacent team in the house.

Growing product engineers
We are all people making stuff together. Some of have different specialties and get the final say in specific matters. Many of are concerned, involved, and contributing to matters in which we don’t have the final say. That’s how multidisciplinary teams collaborate on shared goals.

Kindling a product mindset in your company goes beyond hiring and training product managers: It also means cultivating product engineers. There are a lot of great engineers out there who are not product engineers, and that’s fine. It’s great, actually. Some work on embedded systems. Some deal with civil infrastructure. Some design roller coasters. The list is endless.

Product engineers make products. They care about the product they are making. They understand user experience even if they are not designers. They think about the customer journey even though they are not researchers or marketers. They write, test, and deploy code that runs user-facing software for people, and it shows.

Key takeaways
Product people need first-class technical chops and fluency with the software materials they work on.
This includes comfort with data, science, statistics, and probability.
Product people are experimenters, and use data to find pathways to better customer experiences.
Great product people know how to persuade, cajole, inspire, and lead developers without being their boss.
Lean, agile, iteration on coherent achievable goals can move mountains.
Product engineers are product people too.

# Growing Product People

However product people may see themselves, a lot of the folks they work with, especially the creative types (designers and programmers), view them as “suits.” They’re the business drones. The ones who like meetings.

In your quest for artistic and scientific-minded product people, don’t overcorrect and forget the “general manager” mindset that functions almost as a sort of superego, weighing the competing forces and arriving at a viable path forward.

Sniffing out market opportunities
The earliest-stage product skillsets in the business world deal with market research and discovering opportunities where there is a “job to be done” out there that can be done cheaply, more easily, or otherwise more valuably than the way people are getting that job done today.

In an established business or market niche, this capability can help extend a product to a new market segment, expand it into a platform, or determine a related product to launch to further improve the “user journey” the core product supports.

In startups this is often the foundation work of validating the need for a product, service, or consulting business in a given market space at all, and there are as many ways to do that as there are forms of social science and economic research.

One way I saw this done was at CloudOn, a collaborative gesture-based document editor for tablets and phones (a company that we ultimately sold to Dropbox). My boss there, Jay Zaveri, believed long before the Microsoft Office or Google Docs teams shipped iPad apps that people would like to be more productive on their tablet, and respond to and edit their cloud documents on the go.

To test this hypothesis, he bought some search text ads with a simple message along the lines of “Coming Soon! Edit your Word docs on the iPad” with a call-to-action link saying something like “Sign up to be notified.”

Now you could say this was advertising vaporware, but the idea that a way to edit Word documents on a touch-based tablet might be coming soon motivated thousands, eventually tens of thousands to sign up to be among the first to know.

Naturally, when CloudOn finally launched its 1.0 version in the App Store, this mailing list was notified and the ensuing rush to download the (free) software propelled it to the top 10 app list that day, which itself has a knock-on effect of driving thousands of additional downloads and installations organically.

Growing and sustaining a market win into a business
Having found product-market fit is an achievement worth celebrating. Without it, your product is really nowhere, but it’s only a necessary and not sufficient ingredient in turning a valuable product into a sustainable business.


Differentiation usually involves aspects such as price, availability, quality and reliability.
Product people need to have an eye for “differentiators” — significant differences from the competition to stand out and make switching worthwhile. Note that the competition is frequently not another software product at all but rather the existing de factor manual process used to get that job done.


Gmail’s first logo
When Google launched Gmail, the unlimited storage and threaded message display together represented much more than a small tweak to the preceding email experience. It felt like a leap forward. In the early days you had to be invited to beta test the new mail program and people were clamoring for those invitations out of a sense of envy toward the people already living in the future. Why can’t my mail be that easy, uncluttered, clear, etc.?

A small incremental improvement over the alternative is not a differentiator, as it rarely justifies the effort of migrating one’s data and habits to a new environment for a minor convenience.

A really strong differentiator is sometimes referred to as a “moat” in the medieval sense of an impassible barrier protecting your outer walls. I’ve often heard advantages described as moats (“they’ll never be able to catch up on the research we did / all that data we’ve collected / the network effects of our huge user base”) but have never been told exactly what makes one defense impenetrable and another vulnerable.

Does Facebook have too many users to ever lose its monopoly on Internet sociality? Did MySpace?

GM thinking
You want your product people to be able to think like general managers because they will be the one person in many situations who can see the whole picture and has the ultimate responsibility to keep it thriving.

Operationally, this translates into setting goals, inspiring teams to focus on solving problems and achieving outcomes, measuring results carefully without fear or favor, and making critical decisions based on the available feedback and evidence to provide direction and drive the strategy forward.

Nobody gets into a C suite without having heard “You can’t manage what you can’t measure” (or its variants) a thousand times or more. Because it’s true. It’s every bit as true for business goals and product strategy as it is for spoilage.

Part of this comes down to determining the critical metrics needed to steer by. They need to be captured with high fidelity, benchmarked, and then studied closely as they evolve. Your product people should be able to get down in the weeds with data and pursue all sorts of explanations and hypotheses, and at the same time, the whole company should be aware of one or two, three at the most “North Star metrics” that best represent progress toward the top goals of the quarter or year (or whatever other cycle you plan on).

If your company has embraced the Objectives and Key Results (OKR) method of defining Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), tracking them, and using them to measure success and learn from failure. be very careful about how a companywide OKR regime can make ordinary reporting and reviews more complex. OKRs are not supposed to be used to evaluate performance, but in practice they ofter are. Wherever you stand on the proper use of OKRs, be sure the whole company is on the same page and understands what to expect, if you want to avoid a lot of unnecessary anxiety.

Product OKRs generally involve user engagement, behavior, or revenue data, and rely on well executed “product instrumentation” (the use of a tracking tool or convention such as Google Analytics, Mixpanel, or Amplitude) to provide accurate insight into empirical customer behavior when interacting with your product.

Great product people swim in this analytical data, set up dashboard and alerts, make reports for the rest of the company, post critical data updates in slack channels, and they notice when something unusual is happening in the data before anyone else does.

Unknown unknowns
Most product planning tends to address known challenges and opportunities.

The competitor has a better feature and you might want to invest in reaching parity with them on that function.

Data analyses and interviews tell your product people that customers would like some enhancements to a feature they already love a lot or that they are impatient with a bug that has gone unaddressed for a long time.

The school year starts in the fall so the new product for students needs to be advertised in late summer. We should outsource the maintenance of this legacy product to an external engineering team. Things like that.

But there is another source of challenge and opportunity that may present new information and circumstances to you at any time. Essentially: externalities that are beyond your control. This can range to what used to more of be called “acts of God” such as floods, famines, earthquakes, World Cup victories, and so on, to more mundane unexpected news such as your leading competitor closing a venture round or the suicide of a pop star driving a wave of new users into your mental health product’s community.

At 7 Cups we had a saying: “The problem is the path.” It meant that if we faced our problems squarely and studied the reality of the situation deeply, we would find the solutions we needed. (This worked, like 80% of the time.)

It’s tempting to ignore such unpredictable externalities because, well, they’re unpredictable, but the best product people stay aware that a substantial part of any future being planned for will surprise us, and that the best plans remain flexible and resilient and have contingencies as well as general principles and practices about how to respond in the face of new circumstances whether adverse or opportunistic.

At the PathCheck Foundation, the software we shipped initially in April used GPS protocols to assist contact tracers in a privacy-preserving way to more efficiently and accurately gather potential exposure data from people diagnosed with Covid-19 who volunteered to participate. Several jurisdictions launched with this platform. At the same time the R&D team was exploring many other sensor models, including blue tooth and wifi signals, among others.

Then, in the early summer, Google and Apple announced a partnership to develop and publish a set of “exposure notification” protocols that would rely entirely on Bluetooth and that could not be used in any product that captured location data. Their teams even reached out to ours and told us that our “thought leadership” around surveillance-free digital contact tracing had inspired some of the privacy-preserving architectural choices in their protocols and APIs.

For PathCheck, this meant we needed to pivot!

As much as we believed in the value of our GPS-based solution and were committed to supporting the partner jurisdictions launching with it, we had to recognize that the makers of the two dominant mobile app device operating systems represented the mother of all 800 lb gorillas, and that they were going to strongly determine the direction of the market we were playing in from that point forward.

So, we engaged deeply with Google and Apple, helped many jurisdictions navigate the shifting technical and political waters to determine what solution worked best for them, and developed a Google-Apple Exposure Notification (GAEN) product combining the best aspects of our product discovery and user experience design with the new realities of the bluetooth-based exposure notifications model.

What to expect in a roadmap
As business leader, you want to see the product roadmap. This is natural. Where is the product headed? Is it on track? What are we getting in upcoming releases? Have they fixed my pet peeve bug yet? These are all natural questions. Unfortunately, this can sometimes lead to product teams presenting to you release plans and calling them roadmaps.

To be clear, both things are good and necessary business communications artifacts!

A release plan is something a project manager or release manager should be able to maintain. It provides a schedule of upcoming releases with some indication of what changes to expect in each release. It is needed to coordinate marketing and advertising, to make deadlines for commitments to partners, to launch during the right time of year, to beat the competition. All the things schedules are good for.

A roadmap is not a schedule, though, or should not be. It’s a plan for the way ahead. It can be loosely correlated with time frames, of course, but again those details find more accurate expression in a release plan anyhow.

The roadmap is your product team’s primary artifact reflecting the current product strategy, which itself should be an expression of the product-dependent aspects of the business strategy for the current time period (quarter, fiscal year, ten-year plan).

The best kind of roadmap is organized by objectives (mapped back to OKRs, ideally), often structured along tracks where successive advances toward an objective can be prioritized, with broad time frames such as Now, Next, and Later (my favorite way to do it).


Here is the public-facing roadmap (or the top of each column, at least, for Prodpad, a roadmap product for product managers. How meta is that?
Now is literally what the team is working on right now. What is being built and shipped, what is being researched and defined. It can often loosely be thought of as “this quarter” although that is sometimes less true around the change of quarter.

Next is what the team hopes to move onto when the things they are doing now are done. Most of them will be further steps along the tracks mentioned above. Agile iterative processes mean that it’s not aways clear in advance what the specific ideas involved in that further step will be, but steering closer to the track’s objective is the driving force in making those choices when the time comes. Some of the work that the product team is leading “now” is preparation for work that the dev team will be leading “next.” This can loosely be mapped to the next few quarters, say three to nine months out.

Later is an aspiratonal parking lot of great ideas for where to go after all the things that the team wants to get to next are nailed down. The details are even sketchier becuase they really don’t need or deserve too much attention yet. They are to some extent placeholders to remember where the team thought they were headed back when they were first mapping out the future.

As time grinds on and “now” stuff gets done and “next” stuff turns into “now” stuff, some of the “later” stuff of course ends up on-deck under next and starts demanding a bit more refinement and attention, so this is a living evolving document reflecting the best view of the future the product team is able to articulate at this time.

It can drive fantastic conversations but it’s not really good for “why did we miss that ship date?”

Growth
Another critical instinct you want in at least some of your product people is a nose for growth. They need not necessarily be hired or chartered as “growth hackers” but having some of your product people focused like a laser on how to improve your key north star metrics can introduce a helpful tension to planning aligned with goals.

Some of the most common growth metrics involve things such as unique active users in a time period: Daily active users (DAU), weekly active users (WAU), monthly active users (MAU) as well as ratios among the same.

You can also expect the product team to measure user activation and user engagement. Critical to active user growth is to maximize retention (and, thus, on the other side of the coin, minimize churn), and you may also be trying to drive your “bounce rate” (people who visit your product and then leave without engaging at all) as close to zero as possible.

After user growth metrics, the next most common (and in many cases by far the most important) category is revenue metrics. This can be anything from raw transaction, number of paying users or subscribers, versions of all the same retention, churn, re-engagement type metrics used with active user growth, to calculations of customer lifetime value (LTV), customer acquisition cost (CAC), the all important ration of LTV/CAC, as well as old school product metrics such as cost of goods sold (COGS) with its impact on the profit bottom line.

A third category of growth metric is virality. In the customer life cycle how many of the people who become of aware of the product actually discover it, how many of those engage with it at all, how many become active users, how many are retained, how many are loyal, how many are enthusiastic, and what fraction of them are ambassadors or evangelists who are actively promoting your product to new customers (putting people back into the top of this funnel at awareness)?

The coefficient of virality, then, is the average number of referral to new customers times the conversion rate. If you can get this up above 1.0 then your customer base is growing on its own. The higher the viral coefficient, the faster that growth.

In light of the coronavirus, I wonder if we can find another mathematically accurate term for beneficial spread that lacks these epidemiological overtones?


Viral growth (enthusiastic word of mouth backed by a streamlined invitation and signup flow) has spurred Slack to greater and greater height in the enterprise collaboration software space (image from a Josh Constine article on TechCrunch, “Slack’s growth is insane, with daily user count up 3.5X in a year”).
Instigators vs sustainers
One last note about product people as business operators: Some product people are better suited to starting new things and others are better at taking a going concern and bringing out the best in it. Both types are needed.

Instigators love launching stuff. They make great serial entrepreneurs. They are full of creative hypotheses and their bias to action tends to be maxed out. They also get bored once problems are solved and it’s time to make that second million. Keep giving them new horizons to conquer!

Sustainers are equally valuable. The instigators may shake their heads wondering how someone could stand the boredom of growing a successful product into an even more successful one with a long lifespan. But sustainers find this to be the most satisfying work. Like sailing a well built ship efficiently over the seas, as opposed to building a raft in the middle of an ocean. Put these people to work optimizing your core products.

All good things come to an end, and one of the less glamorous but no less critical responsibilities of product is pulling the plug on features, product, product lines, and even businesses that have outlived their usefulness. I guess we could call product people who specialize in this “undertakers”?

Key Takeaways
The business of product people is business.
Identifying and meeting the needs of markets is a core product skill.
Competitive differentiators and moats can turn a product into a going business.
OKRs help align product roadmap and execution with business strategy.
Keep the product team focused on north star metrics.
You can’t plan for the unknown but someone has to.
Roadmaps are not release plans.
Growth is usually a primary product business goal.
Some product people are better at starting things and others are better at keeping them going.
