# Growing Product People

Product people work with large teams who generally do not report to them. How do they motivate and organize, inspire and lead teams who work in different ways for different bosses? It comes down to communication, empathy, and other so-called “soft skills” (or emotional intelligence).

When I joined 7 Cups as head of product the team was so small that for the first year or so I experienced something few product managers ever do: engineers who reported directly to me. This was a true product development team that ran the gamut from problem space discovery to shipping code. It was also, to being with, just three of us. I deliberately avoided using my VP title because I was also the only product manager, the only user researcher, the only UX designer, and the only data analyst. By the time we had built a mature engineering team with seven or eight developers it was past time for Eng to stand on its own and go toe-to-toe with me. The rare period of product and engineering unity couldn’t last, but it was glorious while it did.

Most product managers depend on engineers who do not report to them to write the code necessary to meet the requirements in their specs. They don’t accomplish this by bossing people around or answering their questions with “because I say so.” If the process is to succeed it is inherently collaborative, involve give-and-take and more often than not requires the product person to go the extra mile to understand the temperament, preferences, favorite issues, and fears of the engineers they work with.

The more collaborative the relationship between product and engineering, the more likely it is that the developers become product people themselves over time.

Multidisciplinary teams
Building great products requires orchestration. Teams bring together varying skill sets, experiences, points of view, incentives, and cultures. There’s a truism in tech that the ultimate experience customers have in your product ends up being a reflection of the team the build it. If the team is fragmented, the user experience will be too. If the team if in conflict, the product will show it. If the team learns to work together, respect each other’s points of view, and find common purpose, the product will sing.

This sort of orchestration doesn’t just happen. It requires a steady cadence of communication and constant readjustment to keep everyone in alignment, troubleshooting problems, and a sixth sense for when things are not really going as smoothly as they appear on the surface.

It also means overcommunication, especially in environments that involve remote work, which at the time I am writing this include basically all software development and which will likely to be the case for a large portion of the technology world for the foreseeable future.

What is overcommunication?
What do I mean by overcommunication? Isn’t overdoing anything bad? Like oversharing? The point is that without consciously erring on the side of opting to communicate as frequently as possible and in every way possible, long-established habits will lead you to undercommunicate when it matters most. You need to commit, as an organization to thorough communication in multiple modes and across many mutually reinforcing channels.

It also means not being passive. Not assuming word will get around. Not forgetting to keep people up to date. Not allowing cliques and claques and cabals to form. It means both a greater commitment to formal communications including quarterly roadmap updates, release notes, sprint retrospectives, newsletter-like team updates, “how to work with us” guidance in a wiki or other documentation, monthly product all-hands, participatory exercises for discovery of problem spaces, generating potential solutions, and aligning around priorities.

Much of this needs to be done formally include everyone. I’m reminded of an anecdote a friend told me about a meeting at her company in a conference room where some of the attendees connected remotely via video. As the meeting broke up, she and a colleague walked back to their cubicles and started a side conversation that was a continuation of one of the topics discussed in the meeting.

Her co-worker suggested they “hold that thought” and continue the conversation via Slack or Hipchat or whatever enterprise chat software their team used at the time, to explicitly include the remote members of the team and avoid inadvertently and unintentionally excluding them from an important discussion.

Empathy
It also involves understanding motivations of teams or individuals who are blocking each other. Unless your enterprise is dysfunctional, it’s best to assume that everyone is doing their best to work together in good faith. When projects founder in the face of misalignment, pushback, or naysaying, product people need to take the time to deeply understand what is driving the conflict.


Empathy is an innate human trait that some people have more of a temperamental capacity for without prompting but empathy can also be developed and tools such as an Empathy Map such as this version produced by Dave Gray for his Gamestorming book represents a good example of a process for systematically putting yourself in someone else’s shoes to understand what is driving them.
A good question I ask when someone appears to be resisting without articulating why is “What are they afraid of?” I don’t mean this in a condescending or superior sense. I literally mean what is it that is frightening them? Are they worried the desirable solution is going to interfere with some aspect of their existing work that they particularly value? Does this ideas closely resemble (to them) one that went awry in the past? Do they perhaps have good product instincts themselves, instincts that are telling them something about the idea of the moment won’t work?

If so, it is worth it to take the time to get to the bottom of these concerns. Take them seriously. Face them head on. Respect the point of view even if it isn’t widely shared, and find a way forward that acknowledges and meets the challenges driving the fear or concern.

Putting things in writing
So product people need to be good communicators, for all the many reasons touched throughout this book so far. Much of that communication takes place in the form of writing. For individual contributor product managers this likely involves requirements documents, specifications, reports, proposals, analyses, slack and email messages, comments on tickets. Product people in other roles may not generate this same volume of written content, but the need to be crisp, unambiguous, and clear remains. Product engineers need to write clear comments in their code and on their commits and pull requests. They also need to respond to comments on tickets and discuss nuanced technical issues in text chats.

Product designers need to annotate their mockups, make presentations with written components, and learn to defend and articulate the values of their designs in words.

It’s not that these folks need to become prose stylists or start writing poetry in their spare time, but it’s important to recognize that using the “technology” of writing is a key craft skill at all levels for product professionals.

An ability to manage up
If you’re a CEO this may be somewhat hard to hear but there are times when people on your team need to manage you. By this I don’t mean that they will try to manipulate you or that you don’t know your own business. It’s just that anyone executing for you is going to try to focus your attention where it can do the most good.

Product leadership at the highest level involves deep, close collaboration with the CEO. (In fact, as I’ll get into in the next chapter, in the early stage of a product organization, most likely the CEO themselves is the one providing product leadership.) This can mean sometimes making strong arguments against the gut instincts of the boss, and it often requires laying out the pros and cons, the benefits and the opportunity costs of competing options that each on their own face feel extremely desirable but which cannot all happen, definitely not all at once.

A good product leader should be able to help you understand difficult and disappointing truths without feeling misled or betrayed.

This skill comes in handy all the way up and down the line. It draws on the exact same hard and soft skills, communication and otherwise, that help build horizontal alignment across product efforts. This is the vertical form of that same coherency-building skillset.

I remember sitting in on an excellent class Luke Wroblewski taught when he was a senior designer at Yahoo (he is now a senior product executive at Google) about influence. Some of it dealt with how design skills can be used to communicate, defend ideas, and make data and arguments visible. But the thing that really stuck in my mind was the notion that you can only influence the people who you work with directly and at most one or two hops away from that.

This is true in all of these directions. Horizontally you can have great influence on the people you work with daily and some influence on those they work with and people you encounter less frequently. Similarly, when you have direct reports you obviously influence them and likewise your relationship with your boss (if you have one, or your board or investors if you don’t) doesn’t extend much past a skip-level. The key insight he offered was that this is true for everyone. Even a CEO has trouble influencing people multiple steps below them in an HR ladder.

Because of this the communication, consensus building, and frank problem solving skills of a product person come in handy at every level, both in binding collaborators together on projects as well as communication expectations and reporting progress and setbacks up the chain.

Key takeways
Product people are responsible for the success of teams without the authority to boss those teams around.
This requires collaboration and orchestration skills that accommodate a wide variety of perspectives, skills, experiences, and even jargon.
Product people dedicate a large portion of their energy to communication and overcommunication.
They also need to develop their empathy in order to effectively finesse the forces that pull different team members in multiple directions.
Product people are good at writing. They have to be.
Product people know how to manage up.

# Growing Product People

Most of the traits and sensibilities of product people discussed so far focus on Doing the Thing Right. None of that matters, though, without an ability to focus the team and Doing the Right Thing.

Products are more than just a bundle of features and bug fixes, and roadmaps are more than just giant wishlist.

The product practice sits at the nexus of every input into, and every stakeholder of, the customer experience that represents your company’s brand. Product people shoulder the difficult but critical responsibility of weighing the opportunities (and risks) and forging a consensus that moves the team forward and helps deliver valuable outcomes.

There will always be a surplus of great ideas, new problems, ongoing unaddressed issues, deep-pocketed partners, eager sales efforts, business fads, and competitive setbacks.

There will always be too many good ideas to do them all at once, without even getting into the opportunity costs involved in each or the fact that many good ideas are mutually incompatible.

For example, you can’t have two top priorities. You can have any number of important things that you think matter but only one of them can be the top priority (and if not, then you have zero top priorities, not two).

Weighing these priorities, wishes, good ideas, problems, etc. against each other is a fundamental product practice that takes place at every level of the business. Whoever speaks for product in the C-suite will ultimately decide what the entire product org or effort focuses on, what gets funded, who gets hired, headcount, and so on.

Product leadership needs to address and respond to the business strategy through a product roadmap and operational plan. Product practitioners need to make hard choices about what to ship, what to work on next, the order of the backlog, the priority of bugs, and so on.

Prioritization methods
One sign you’ve found a product person is that they are tackling prioritization in a systematic way. There is no one single ideal prioritization method or process or exercise that will suit every occasion and context, but product people realize that there are always too many good ideas to go around, that stakeholders with varying degree of insight and claim over outcomes all need to be understood, respected, and addressed, and that a transparent open process for weighing and evaluating priorities works best.

The most common prioritization methods involve weighing the effort to execute an idea against the potential impact of doing so. In its simplest form, all ideas can be rated as high or low effort and then high or low impact. This enables a first-order rough sort. High impact, low effort things go first! Low impact, high effort things happen never. The other two categories then require more careful orchestration to balance the longer-term and larger efforts that may yield profoundly good results against the drumbeat of relatively trivial or tactical tasks that are easy to do but won’t in and of themselves “move the needle.”

The next level of sophistication for that process is to use t-shirt sizes for estimates (small, medium, and large or S, M, L) which gives you another point on each scale. Most commonly a large surface such as a wall is cleared to enable an X and Y axis with Y (vertical) for impact and X (horizontal) for effort. The same logic applies at all of these scales: the stuff in furthest in the high impact/low effort corner comes first, and it’s the diagonal in the middle that requires the most scrutiny.

For more complex decisions, frameworks such as RICE (reach, impact, confidence, and effort). This model keeps the concept of effort and impact from the simple one above but also give you a variable to scope the reach of the idea (is it going to impact all of your customers all the time? new users? a subset of existing users? etc.) as well a variable to capture how confident the estimate (or the consensus of the group) is, relative to other ideas.

This latter factor helps address the subjectivity of a quasi-algebraic scoring model such as RICE, but ironically does so by introducing an additional subjective variable.

I am quite fond of the Eisenhower matrix, with which most executives are familiar. It is a two-by-two grid that divides tasks, projects, or goals first by whether they are urgent or not, and then by whether they are important or not. The great advantage of this analysis is that it prevents you for confusing urgency for importance (and vice versa).

It is far too easy for any of us at any level of an organization to become reactive to urgent interruptions, emergencies, crisis, deadlines, launch dates, publicity schedules, quarterly reports, and so on. It is not possible to avoid urgent priorities and of course most urgent things need to be addressed, but not everything that’s urgent is important, and not every urgent thing needs to be handled at the highest level.

More critically, there are many important things that are not urgent in the sense that they are not due immediately and they are not blocking anything right now but being postponed, but the great risk is that these things never get addressed (or, more likely, only ever get addressed when they become urgent, generally by way of catching on fire).

This analysis reminds us to plan and schedule longer term and slower-burning matters so that they come to fruition when they are needed and we escape the “take a crisis number” method of planning that is more akin to a panic attack than a sustainable execution process.

The Eisenhower matrix reminds you to do those things that are urgent and important immediately, and decide when important things that are not urgent need to happen (schedule them). It tells you to delegate the things that are urgent but not important to us directly, and to delete (forget about) the things that are neither urgent nor important enough to ever be worthy of your attention.


The famous Eisenhower Matrix helps you plan ahead for important things that are not literally on fire at right this exact moment.
Radical focus
It goes beyond prioritization of one goal over another, one feature over another, one market over another. A core mission of product people is to maintain focus, to avoid spreading everyone too thin chasing distractions. This is not always a welcome voice in the room. The product person takes away the punchbowl right when the party is getting going.

Research by NYU and University of Hamburg psychology professor Gabriele Oettingen has shown that daydreaming about the future, merely imagining the outcomes you are hoping to achieve, by itself make you less likely to accomplish those goals. This may seem counterintuitive to any of us raised on the power of positive thinking, vision boards, or The Secret.

Apparently, the problem is that once your brain has “seen” this vision of the happy outcome, it naturally (I mean literally, in a chemical sense) relaxes and feels less motivated to do the work necessary to get there. It’s the opposite of delay gratification.

Fortunately, there is a way to escape this trap. There is nothing inherently wrong with envisioning desirable outcomes (or with daydreaming for that matter). The trick is to go beyond this and to spend time thinking thoroughly and unflinchingly about what obstacles are likely to prevent you from achieving this outcome. Then you make plans for how you will overcome these obstacles and at this point we’re into the operationalization of any process, but the point remains that the key moment involves honestly assessing the challenges, risks, and potential downsides of the idea under consideration.

Radical candor
Product people say “Wait!” They say “What about…?” they say “We have to stop this thing many of us love” and they say “No” even to you, the boss.

Now, if you, the boss, overrule your head of product, or if the consensus of a leadership team is to do so, they will “disagree and commit” and do everything possible to make the idea work. They won’t drag their feet to sandbag the idea or say “I told you so” if it fails. They aren’t afraid to take a position because they are not afraid of being wrong or of “losing.” They will give you their best possible advice and let the chips fall.

No optional complexity
One of the things I’ve learned shipping code for several decades is that every project is plenty complex enough from the get go. Building software on the internet (“in the cloud”) for mobile users migrating across multiple device platforms and operating systems and client applications is an inherently complex exercise that also requires orchestrating the efforts of multiple teams trained in entirely different disciplines and schools of thought.

So why make it harder?

I’ve learned that if there is anything you don’t have to do, don’t do it. I call this rule “No optional complexity.” In my dad’s generation they had a version of this insight they called KISS (“Keep it simple, stupid!”). That was more about not overthinking things but I feel it comes from the same wellspring of wisdom.

Of course, this is easier said than done, and frankly always easier to recognize in hindsight, after you’ve once again insisted on including a feature or pursuing an initiative that wasn’t really necessary. So why is it so hard to recognize that once again complexity is multiplying like the enchanted brooms of the sorcerer’s apprentice?


Mickey had an OKR about keeping things tidy. Then he decided to use regular expressions. Now he has two problems.
It’s because complexity can occur at any scale, in any aspect of your work. It can have to do with trying to market to too many different types of customers all at once. It can involve going after a consumer market and a B2B market at the same time. It can involve (it usually does) shipping way too many B-features instead of one A+ feature. It can involve how to build and staff your teams.

When I was directing the product team at CloudOn, a productivity startup making a collaborative product for editing cloud-stored documents on gesture-based mobile devices, we made the decision to port our iOS product (which initially ran just on iPads) to Android tablets. We reasoned that we could extend our market to the much larger Android base worldwide and that we could reuse so much of research and discovery and design work, as well as the platform and R&D technology that went in the iOS instance that “fast following” our Apple releases with near-parity versions for Android should be a relatively efficient use of our engineering resources.

In retrospect, in the long run, I believe this was a big mistake. We never reached nearly the same number of users on the Android platform, which is itself a severely fragmented ecosystem of devices running many different version of the operating system. Furthermore, the anticipated efficiencies never materialized to anything like the degree expected. (It was probably more like 180% of the work to release on both platforms than the 120% we had been projecting.) The devil is always in the details and the unique aspects of the Android OS perpetually demanded extra attention and testing. Worse, Android native users could tell our app was a port from iOS. It didn’t feel like a native Android experience. Addressing those subtleties also took time and never fully met expectations.

We could have saved ourselves a great deal of grief and met some of our goals a lot more easily by focusing entirely on the iOS universe until we had that really nailed.

The other thing that makes recognizing this problem hard is that some forms of complexity are needed either to achieve the valuable experience you are trying to offer or to stretch towards your goals in an achievable way.

In the exact same context at CloudOn we made another decision that I stand by to this day. We debated intensely about whether to offer a document editor (like Word or Google Docs) for the phone form factor. It wasn’t clear to us that people really wanted to edit, comment on, or otherwise work on documents on their small screens.

We even spent some time prototyping and testing workflows that we felt made the most sense when mobile, such as approving edits and replying to comments and of course reading drafts. What surprised us once we did ship the iPhone version of the CloudOn app was that people were absolutely willing to edit documents (and even spreadsheets) on their tiny little screens. Almost the exact same percentage of sessions involved editing. Now it’s possible that the editing done on phones was more light editing than that done on tablets, but the end result was that the experience didn’t need to be significantly different on the much smaller screen (mostly navigation had to be hidden away), and the added “complexity” of supporting phones in addition to tablets was affordable and worthwhile.

Difficult choices
In the end it all comes down to difficult choices. The easy ones take care of themselves. The tough ones involve deciding between two or more things you want when you can only have one, or choosing which punishment to take when it’s impossible to avoid the hit one way or another.

Beyond having the expertise, analytical skill, and perspicacity necessary to weigh and measure hard choices and recommend what to do, a product person needs to be able to bring everyone else along with them. They can’t dictate the decision most of the time. They need everyone to buy in, most of all leadership.

I’ve used the word estimate or estimation here when talking about prioritization and it’s worth noting that for many engineers (and product people) the whole concept of estimation is fraught with peril. It’s a minefield that in many organizations devolves into sandbagging or death marches.

I’m a fan of Tom Kerwin’s concept of pivot triggers (with its wonderful IF BS STOP mnemonic), which suggests framing the need to weigh and balance effort in terms of “investment” (instead of the usual estimation frame), saying

Your engineers are probably familiar with the debate around estimation. That’s where you think about what you want to build, and then estimate how long it’ll take to get it all done. The unspoken assumptions are often something like “our customers will want this, because it’s obvious to us that they ought to” and “this thing will work in the end, if we keep going long enough.” Once you’re busy working to the plan, the only signal telling you that the plan isn’t working is that vague sense of creeping uncertainty we came across before.

Instead of estimation, think investing. That’s where you ask yourself: what’s going to be better for people in the world after we’ve done this, and how much time and energy is it worth us spending to get to “better”?

You’re more interested in how to know when you’ve done enough. Your focus stays on figuring out how to actually get to “better” from where you are today, not on completing the plan you had a few months ago.

Look for people who can bring together a consensus about difficult choices and channel the energy of the team on a focused goal. Those are your product people.

Key takeaways
Product management involves constantly choosing among possible directions.
Prioritization is a skill that product offers up and down the org chart, especially when planning, setting OKRs, and making roadmaps.
Product people help keep everyone focused and face the challenges that need to be overcome without flinching
Complexity will always find its way into your work so avoid adding any optional complexity.
Rely on product people to help make the most difficult decisions about where to invest your precious resources.

# 10 min read
# Draft in
# Growing Product People

How do you create a culture that fosters and grows product people? Who are the product people you’ve already got (regardless of title)? Which of your talent can become product people? How do you hire and build a product team?

By this time I expect that you have a pretty good feel for what product mindset can do for your business. Cultivating such people so they thrive requires that you invest in a product culture that nurtures and empowers them. So how do you build a product organization, and where do you find these product people?

Building a product organization
Creating a real product organization starts with a commitment from the top, from you. Change is never easy and new habits cause friction when they clash with comforting older customs. Your executive championship of the product mindset will be a necessary, but not sufficient, ingredient in transforming your business and empowering your product people.

Next, you’re going to need to do the equivalent of a “build vs. buy” analysis that will depend a lot on your current situation.

Do you have a product org already, but is it what Marty Cagan calls a “feature factory” stuck in what Melissa Perri called “the build trap”?
Do you ship software using agile methods with a lot of people called product owners or product managers but no coherent product strategy or roadmap?
If either of the above are true, then you’ll need to bring in new product leadership who will then empower the existing team or make staffing adjustments over time to enable this.

Do you build software currently without using a product model and without product roles on your teams?
Are you on the verge of making your first product hire?
Have you hired a head of product who has been unable to build a product practice and execute?
In these scenarios, you still have the opportunity to build a product org from scratch. The next step is to determine whether you’ve already got talent on staff prepared to take on product responsibilities, either by bringing a product mindset to their existing role or by shifting formally into a product management job.

Investing in your team
To begin with, you need to invest in product craft. Product people need to master these skills and techniques discussed throughout this book, and you need to help them do that. If you have senior product leadership in place (more on that in a moment) then it will be their responsibility to put in place the educational opportunities and events, the training programs, and to build an operation that provides career growth and professional development for product practitioners.

Product training should be offered to any practitioner who is interested, not just to people with product manager job titles. The goal is to nurture and validate product mindset in any role, across the entire organization.


Atlassian puts on an internal product craft conference annually to train product people and build the product practice community across the company.
If you do not yet have senior product leadership doing this, then the responsibility will fall to you until that role is filled. (It may make sense to work with a product consultant or a temporary head of product to jumpstart this process.)

A reliable way to broaden and strengthen product skills across your org is through cross-training. This depends on having at least one person who has mastered the skill that others are hoping to develop. Let’s take for example, tracking product analytical data, such as retention, and working systematically to improve it.

This is an activity that involves a handful of techniques and skills that can be learned, among them:

capturing valid meaningful data
manipulating charts and graphs to analyze the data
engaging in discovery to understand what is going with customers that might explain the data
developing hypotheses about what might improve the experience
prioritizing experiments to test these hypotheses
learning from the outcomes of experiments and feeding this learning back into the discovery process
The best way to cross-train a product person who is more junior or who has less experience with these activities is to pair them with a senior product person who excels at these things. The key part is that the person who is learning does the work. The person who has the wisdom and experience provides coaching, guidance, and feedback.

In the short term, these tasks will take a bit longer than if they were assigned to the experienced hand, but the benefit to distributing this expertise more widely on the team outweighs this cost, and the more cross-training goes on, the more supple and agile the product team will be, more able to flex, recombine, and adapt to handle changing circumstances.

Enabling transitions
When you are growing a product team out of the “soil” of your existing staff, you will want to identify people who show the sort of aptitude and instincts discussed throughout this book. They may be engineers, designers, marketers, business analysts, project managers, sales people, customer support staff, or really anything.

Make it clear that you are building a product practice and that it will involve some hiring and that people in other roles will be involved as the entire enterprise takes on the habits of a product organization, but that you also welcome people formally transitioning into product management roles, and will facilitate this by providing training, coaching, and mentorship.

Hiring product people
When a business that has never had product managers before is on the verge of hiring their first product manager (or head of product), there can be a lot of confusion about where to start, what level of talent to bring on first, and how to start incorporating product management into existing processes.

There are two main variants of this junction in a company’s life:

An established business that has been making and shipping software for years now and has not until now seen any need for or benefit from including product managers in the software development process.
An early stage startup that consists primarily of one or two founders, a roomful of software engineers (possibly off-shore), and perhaps a handful of other operational roles.
The first scenario depends upon organizational change, and while it can be kindled at first with mid-level or junior product managers, the building of a truly empowered product organization won’t really get going without senior product leadership (either on staff or consulting).

For a startup, this can be an anxiety-filled junction, when the time comes to hire a first product person. The vast majority of my own consulting engagements these days are with startups that are literally trying to work out just exactly what product even is, when they need to hire their first product person, what level that person ought to be, and how to find them.

The truth is that in the early stages of a new startup, the founders need to “own” the product. I’d be somewhat suspicious of a founder who planned to outsource the core product work when getting a new business off the ground. But the time always comes when the CEO or operational founder has their hands full with too many things (hiring, dealing with investors or a board, sales, people operations, and so on) and need the leverage that only a dedicated product person can provide.

This is like a young parent getting a babysitter for the first time: terrifying. It only works if there is deep trust, understanding, and simpatico. A CEO who is lying awake worrying that their new head of product “doesn’t really get it” or might not be paying attention to the right things is getting less than no benefit from having delegated that work. What she needs is someone she fully believes does “get it,” is executing the agreed upon product strategy, and knows when to escalate tricky matters to the boss so that there are never any unpleasant surprises.

Product people are well aware of how challenging this first hire can be. Just the other day an entrepreneur I worked with years ago at Yahoo, Greg Cohn, joked to me that “everybody knows you want to be the second product hire.” And I knew just what he meant. There seem to be no end to businesses looking for an interim VP of Product to help from manage the transition period after the first candidate hired as a product head has washed out.

How senior the first product hire?
Another tricky aspect of the first product hire is determining the level of experience needed. In early stage startups it’s dangerous to make things too top-heavy, so it’s not uncommon to start with a junior or intermediate level product manager to help more with coordination and alignment and less with strategy and practice building. This can be a good way for the company to start learning product habits and sketching out operational processes that will be formalized over time as the teams evolve.

In more mature organizations, it’s critical to bring in senior product leadership. Titles vary, but tend to be one of the following

head of product — typically used by companies trying to keep things ambiguous and flexible in terms of level
director or senior director of product — tends to be a middle manager
VP (or SVP) of product — should have P&L responsibility
Chief product officer — represents the product org as a peer of other major business functions
The meanings and levels of these titles vary widely from company to company, and tend to map to equivalent HR levels for similar titles in other practices. Putting the specific titles that make the most sense in your organization to one side, the key question is where the buck stops and who speaks for product at the highest levels.

So, for example, you’re going to have a chief product officer. You may not hire anyone with that title, but if you don’t then the most senior product person will not be in the C suite and will report to someone who is. This may be the CEO, the COO, the CTO, or even some other role (less likely), but in any of those cases that person is de facto the chief product officer and will have the responsibility of championing the product culture.

If you do have a CPO, then that person is of course also the head of product and its redundant to give someone else that title (unless they are head of product “for” some subset of the product portfolio, say). But if the chief product office has other responsibilities besides product, then you will need a head of product (or VP or director) who will be the highest ranking person on the product staff and work closely with the CEO (or whomever they report to) to drive product strategy and operations.

Product expert (and a mentor of mine) Rich Mironov makes the point that when you are hiring your first head of product they should have experience managing product teams. This may sound dead obvious, but you’d be surprised. As he notes in his article Hiring a Head of Product he notes that the three most common errors he’s encountered in decades of consulting are:

Exec teams don’t know what Heads of Product do, or want someone who will “make development more efficient.”
They don’t value experience running product management teams. Instead, they overweight narrow technical or market segment familiarity
Each department wants to hire in its own image. Sales execs want more sales-y Heads of Product; development execs want more engineering-ish Heads of Product; marketing teams want a marketer in product clothing… no candidate passes muster with all groups.
Leadership teams
A successful first product lead can make progress in building a product team, and growing product people, but as you scale, it will become clear relatively quickly that there are several aspects of leadership that need to be handled, and over time no one single person can field them all. (It’s not so much that no one person is capable of addressing all these aspects. It’s more that there are only so many hours in a day, too much context switching is inefficient, and people need to focus!)

In a recent blog post on maturing design leadership teams, Peter Merholz (a design executive and consultant) made a similar point about design teams (which as we know are closely adjacent to product), parsing the various leadership roles into these categories:

Executive leadership responsible for vision, championship, standards, recruiting (such as a CPO or VP Product).
Operations leadership establishing workflow processes, program management, training and resources (Product Ops).
Directors who manage major groupings of the team as whole, maintain quality, hire for their teams, manage careers (Director of Product).
Principals or high-level individual contributors who demonstrate and spread craft leadership (Group Product Managers, Principal Product Managers).
Filling all these types of roles on day one would be overkill, but as a product org scales, all these aspects of leadership become critical and require many hands to fulfill.

Start with one strong lead, and give them the mandate to build a product culture.

Key Takeaways
Whether building a product org from scratch or transforming a feature factory into an empowered product culture, you may be able to convert some existing staff into product people and you will likely need to hire some new talent as well.
An ongoing commitment to training people in product craft will help build a product mindset across your org.
Recruit product managers from other roles.
Determine how senior your product leadership needs to be at first and who will be championing product management in the C suite.
The first head of product you hire is always a difficult choice, and the risk of failure for anyone attempting to carve out a product practice where none yet exists is always high.
Consider working with a consultant or interim head of product if the perfect leader doesn’t present themselves right when you need them.
Over time you will need to cultivate multiple types of product leaders.
You can do this!

docs.filename: Christian Crumlish Voice & Tone Style Guide.md
docs.content:
# Christian Crumlish Voice & Tone Style Guide

## Overview
This guide captures the distinctive writing style of Christian Crumlish (xian/mediajunkie) for use in generating blog posts, technical communications, and professional content that authentically represents his voice.

## Core Voice Characteristics

### 1. **Conversational Authority**
- Writes with deep expertise but maintains accessibility
- Balances professional knowledge with informal, approachable language
- Uses "you" directly to address readers
- Shares personal anecdotes and experiences to illustrate points

**Example from writing:**
> "So far, the role of a product manager probably sounds pretty good. It may be a bit broad and overleveraged at times, but it does play a pivotal role in what actually gets done..."

### 2. **Self-Aware Humor**
- Frequently uses parenthetical asides for wry observations
- Makes pop culture references (Star Trek, Grateful Dead, Tom Stoppard)
- Self-deprecating when appropriate
- Uses informal contractions and colloquialisms

**Example:**
> "If you've known me for a while, you know I'm into the Dead. One of their best songs asks the question 'Are you kind?'"

### 3. **Bridge-Building Perspective**
- Acknowledges multiple viewpoints ("it depends")
- Addresses potential objections or alternative perspectives
- Uses "we" to create inclusion with readers
- Frames conflicts as opportunities for collaboration

### 4. **Structured Informality**
- Uses lists, subheadings, and clear organization
- Employs rhetorical questions to guide thinking
- Mixes technical precision with conversational flow
- Uses italics for emphasis and *very* specific stress

## Writing Patterns

### Sentence Structure
- **Short punchy statements for emphasis:** "Product managers write a lot."
- **Longer, flowing sentences with multiple clauses** when explaining complex ideas
- **Questions as transitions:** "But what exactly does a PM do all day?"
- **Fragment sentences for impact:** "Very powerful stuff!"
- **Evocative single-sentence paragraphs:** "Nosiness will take you far."
- **Call-and-response patterns:** Setting up and answering your own questions

### Paragraph Flow
- Opens with a clear statement or question
- Develops the idea with examples or explanation
- Often ends with a forward-looking statement or transition
- Uses single-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- Employs visual breaks (centered text, images, diagrams) between sections

### Transitions and Connectors
- "So..." (frequently starts sentences)
- "But..." (often begins contrasting thoughts)
- "OK, but..." (acknowledges then pivots)
- "Mind you..." (adds important caveats)
- "Having said that..." (introduces nuance)

## Technical Writing Approach

### When Explaining Concepts
1. Start with the accessible definition
2. Acknowledge complexity ("This can mean several things...")
3. Use concrete examples
4. Reference authoritative sources with context
5. Circle back to practical application

### Use of Examples
- Personal work experiences (Yahoo, 7 Cups, CloudOn, 18F)
- Specific product scenarios
- Industry figures by name with context
- Pop culture metaphors to clarify concepts

## Characteristic Phrases and Patterns

### Opening Patterns
- "When I was at [Company]..."
- "A few years back..."
- "So here's the thing..."
- "If you're like most [audience]..."
- "When the call came in..."
- "Meanwhile, back at [project/place]..."
- "Let's start by rewinding a bunch of years..."

### Aside Markers
- "(More on this later...)"
- "(Warning: [qualification])"
- "(Spoiler alert: [preview])"
- "— meaning, in short, that..."
- "(I'll ask him to take a look at this before publication)"
- "(or, you know, the more dignified version of that)"

### Narrative Devices
- "Meanwhile, back at..." (returns to ongoing story)
- "Before plowing ahead, let's check in on..."
- "Taking a step back..."
- Section headers like "Put me in, coach"

### Emphasis Techniques
- ALL CAPS for acronym definitions or strong emphasis
- *Italics* for subtle stress or introducing terms
- "Scare quotes" for questionable terminology
- Bold for **key concepts** (sparingly)

## Content Themes

### Recurring Topics
- Product management and UX intersection
- Team dynamics and collaboration
- Public service and civic tech
- Career development and transitions
- Systems thinking and information architecture

### Value Expressions
- User-centered focus
- Pragmatic idealism
- Collaborative problem-solving
- Public service orientation
- Continuous learning

## Tone Variations by Context

### Professional/Educational Content
- More structured, with clear learning objectives
- Still conversational but slightly more formal
- Heavy use of examples and "from the trenches" stories
- Acknowledges different experience levels

### Blog Posts/Reflections
- More personal, includes life details
- References to music, culture, philosophy
- Stream-of-consciousness elements
- Direct address to community/audience

### Technical Explanations
- Patient, step-by-step approach
- Anticipates reader questions
- Uses analogies and metaphors
- Never condescending

## Additional Voice Elements

### Storytelling Techniques
- **Parallel narratives:** Weaving between current insights and past experiences
- **The "meanwhile" device:** Returning to ongoing stories ("Meanwhile, back in Cambridge...")
- **Cliffhangers and callbacks:** Setting up story elements to return to later
- **Self-referential humor:** Comments on the writing process itself

### Visual and Structural Elements
- Strategic use of subheadings as narrative devices
- Centered text for emphasis or transitions
- Diagrams and visual metaphors (even if just described)
- "Key takeaways" sections to summarize learning points
- References to figures/images that illustrate concepts

### Cultural References and Metaphors
- The Grateful Dead (recurring motif)
- Pop culture (Tom Stoppard, Star Trek)
- Physical metaphors ("crawl through glass")
- California/Bay Area geography and culture
- Mix of high and low references

### Humor Styles
- **Self-deprecating:** "Product guy to the stars" followed by explaining it's just an expression
- **Irreverent observations:** "Eng is pronounced enj"
- **Nerd culture references:** "reading xkcd," "devel-random," "l33t dorkiness"
- **Deflating pomposity:** Following serious points with casual asides
- **Running gags:** The ongoing PathCheck narrative, "meanwhile back at..."

### Teaching Voice Markers
- **Foreshadowing:** "We'll come back to that"
- **Direct wisdom:** "Product managers have a saying: 'Good enough is good enough'"
- **Industry insider tips:** Pronunciations, unspoken rules, cultural observations
- **Balanced perspectives:** "It depends on..." followed by specific contexts
- **Demystification:** Breaking down intimidating concepts into relatable pieces

## Voice Registers

### Technical Explanation Mode
- Patient, thorough explanations with multiple entry points
- Anticipates reader confusion: "OK, but why is it called a funnel?"
- Uses physical metaphors for abstract concepts
- Includes specific technical details (MySQL, Kanban, sprint planning)
- Balances precision with accessibility

### Industry Insider Voice
- Drops insider knowledge casually: "Eng is pronounced enj"
- References specific people and their roles at companies
- Shares "what really happens" vs. official processes
- Uses and then immediately explains jargon
- Cultural observations about different tribes (engineers, designers, "suits")

### Meta-Commentary with Wry Edge
- Comments on writing itself: "(grammar wat?)"
- Acknowledges absurdity while taking work seriously
- Makes fun of industry buzzwords while using them
- Self-aware about contradictions and complexity
- Playful resistance to taking things too seriously

## Sample Opening (for comparison)

**In Christian's voice:**
"So I've been thinking about this whole AI-assisted writing thing, and here's where I've landed: it's not about replacing our voices, but about amplifying them. When I was at 18F (and before that at ODI), we talked a lot about capacity building. This feels like that — giving folks tools to do more of what they're already good at, just faster and at scale. Mind you, there's a world of difference between 'sounds like me' and 'sounds like what I'd actually say,' but we're getting closer. Let me show you what I mean..."

This guide should help generate content that captures Christian's distinctive blend of expertise, accessibility, humor, and humanity.

docs.filename: Building Piper Morgan - Episode 2: The Effort/Impact Matrix of AI Writing.md
docs.content:
# Building Piper Morgan - Episode 2: The Effort/Impact Matrix of AI Writing

You can't have two top priorities.

I learned this the hard way at 7 Cups when we were trying to simultaneously build a peer support platform AND a therapist marketplace AND a self-care app. Spoiler alert: we had zero top priorities, not three.

So when I started thinking about where AI writing assistance could actually help versus where it was just shiny-object syndrome, I did what any self-respecting product person would do: I made a 2x2 matrix.

## The Setup

Here's the thing about prioritization methods — they're never perfect, but they force you to think systematically about trade-offs. And with AI tools, the trade-offs are... weird.

Traditional effort/impact analysis assumes effort is about resources: time, money, engineering hours. With AI writing, effort is more like: How much context do I need to provide? How much editing will this require? How likely am I to spend more time fixing it than I would have spent writing it myself?

Impact is weird too. It's not just "will this move the needle on OKRs?" It's also: Will this free me up to do higher-value work? Will this help me maintain consistency across a massive documentation set? Will this let me actually ship on time?

(Yes, I just admitted that shipping on time is sometimes more important than perfection. Sue me.)

## Meanwhile, Back in the Real World...

Let me get concrete. Last week I had:
- A strategy doc that needed writing (high impact, genuinely needed my brain)
- Release notes for a feature update (medium impact, formulaic structure)
- Seventeen Slack messages explaining the same technical concept to different stakeholders (low impact individually, high effort cumulatively)
- A blog post I'd been meaning to write for three months (medium impact, but important for thought leadership)

Old me would have done them in order of deadline panic. New me ran them through the AI assistance matrix.

## The Matrix (No, Not That One)

**High Impact, Low Effort to Augment:**
- First drafts of routine communications
- Expanding bullet points into prose
- Reformatting existing content for different audiences
- Creating templates from examples

This is your sweet spot. Maximum leverage, minimum weirdness.

**High Impact, High Effort to Augment:**
- Complex technical documentation (needs tons of context)
- Strategic narratives (requires too much back-and-forth)
- Anything involving genuine creativity or novel insights
- Responses to sensitive situations

Here's where you need to be honest: is the effort to make AI helpful actually more than just doing it yourself?

**Low Impact, Low Effort to Augment:**
- Routine status updates
- Meeting summaries from notes
- Standard email responses
- Basic formatting tasks

Sure, use AI here. It's not changing the world, but every minute counts.

**Low Impact, High Effort to Augment:**
- Niche technical explanations that require extensive context
- Highly personalized messages
- Anything where your specific voice really matters

Just... don't. This is where AI assistance becomes AI theater.

## What I Actually Did

Remember that list from last week? Here's how it played out:

**Strategy doc:** Wrote it myself, but used AI to help structure my thoughts and create a comprehensive outline. (30% time savings, 100% still my thinking)

**Release notes:** Fed the AI my bullet points and a template from previous releases. Edited the output for accuracy. (70% time savings, good enough is good enough)

**Seventeen Slack explanations:** Created one good explanation with AI help, then customized it slightly for each audience. (80% time savings, plus bonus consistency)

**Blog post:** You're reading it. I wrote the opener and the framework, AI helped me flesh out the middle sections, I rewrote the conclusion. (50% time savings, and I actually shipped it)

## The Part Nobody Talks About

Here's what's actually hard about this: you have to be rigorous about tracking what works and what doesn't. It's tempting to use AI for everything (new toy syndrome) or nothing (luddite syndrome).

I started keeping a simple log:
- Task type
- Time estimated without AI
- Time actually spent (including AI wrangling)
- Quality assessment (did I have to rewrite it?)
- Would I do it again?

After three weeks, patterns emerged that surprised me. My assumptions about what would be easy for AI to help with were often backwards. Turns out it's great at helping me structure complex arguments but terrible at mimicking my sense of humor. (Its jokes are like dad jokes, but somehow worse?)

## The Leadership Angle

If you're thinking about rolling out AI writing tools for your team, here's my advice: start with the effort/impact matrix, but make it collaborative.

Have your team track their own experiments. What works for one person might be a disaster for another. Your principal engineer might find AI helpful for documentation but your UX writer might find it actively harmful for their work.

And please, for the love of all that is holy, don't mandate AI use for everything. That's like mandating that everyone use the same keyboard. Some people need mechanical switches, some people type on their phones, and some weirdos still use Dvorak layouts. (Hi, I see you.)

## Key Takeaways (Because Product Habits Die Hard)

1. **Not all writing tasks are created equal** — categorize before you delegate to AI
2. **Track your actual results** — assumptions about AI effectiveness are often wrong
3. **The sweet spot is augmentation, not replacement** — think bicycle, not car
4. **Different people will have different sweet spots** — what works for me might not work for you
5. **Good enough often IS good enough** — especially for routine communications

## What's Next

Next week I'm going to share my favorite prompting patterns and why "write like Christian Crumlish" is actually terrible instruction for an AI. (Turns out you need to be way more specific about which version of Christian Crumlish you want. Conference speaker Christian? Documentation Christian? 2am Slack rant Christian? They're different people.)

Meanwhile, I'm using this framework to help my team figure out where AI can help them ship better documentation faster without losing their minds or their authentic voices.

Is it perfect? Hell no.
Is it better than either extreme (AI everything or AI nothing)? Absolutely.
Are we all just figuring this out as we go? 100%.

But at least we're doing it systematically. And really, isn't that peak product manager energy?

---

*What's your take on the effort/impact of AI in your workflow? Have you found surprising sweet spots or spectacular failures? Drop me a comment — I'm genuinely curious whether my matrix resonates or if you've found a completely different framework that works better.*

docs.filename: Sample: Building Piper Morgan - Episode 1: Why I'm Teaching My AI to Write Like Me.md
docs.content:
# Building Piper Morgan - Episode 1: Why I'm Teaching My AI to Write Like Me

OK, so here's what I'm doing and why it might sound completely bananas at first: I'm training an AI to write in my voice. Not because I'm lazy (well, not *just* because I'm lazy), but because I think there's something genuinely interesting happening at the intersection of human expertise and AI capability that we're all dancing around but not quite talking about directly.

And before you @ me about "cheating" or whatever — stick with me here. This isn't about replacing human creativity. It's about something weirder and potentially more interesting.

## The Problem Nobody Wants to Admit

When the call came in to help with product documentation for a govtech project (yes, I'm back in the civic tech salt mines and loving it), I found myself staring at the same problem I've had for years: I can either write good documentation or ship good product, but rarely both at the same time.

Sound familiar?

We all know documentation matters. We all swear we'll keep it updated. We all fail spectacularly at this. It's like going to the gym — virtuous in theory, abandoned by February.

But here's the thing that changed: I realized I'd been thinking about AI writing assistance completely backward. I was treating it like a better spell-checker when I should have been treating it like... well, like I treat junior product managers.

## Meanwhile, Back in My Writing Cave...

I've written a book (*Product Management for UX People* — shameless plug, but hey, it's relevant), countless blog posts, documentation that actually got read (I know, right?), and enough Slack messages to fill a medium-sized library. 

My voice is all over the place. It's in git commits, in strategy docs, in rambling emails to colleagues at 2 AM when I suddenly figure out why that feature isn't working.

And I started thinking: What if I could teach an AI not just what I know, but *how I explain what I know*?

(Grammar wat? Yeah, I know. Stick with me.)

## The Experiment Begins

So I started feeding Claude (yes, I name my AI collaborators, fight me) examples of my writing. Not the polished stuff — the raw drafts, the blog posts I never published, the explanations I typed out in Slack when someone asked "but what exactly does a product manager do?"

You know what's wild? Patterns emerged that I didn't even know I had:
- I always start explanations with an accessible hook
- I use "OK, but..." to acknowledge and then reframe objections  
- I drop cultural references like breadcrumbs (apparently I can't go three paragraphs without mentioning the Dead)
- I explain technical concepts using physical metaphors
- My parenthetical asides have parenthetical asides (like this (seriously))

## The "Cheating" Question

Let's address the elephant in the room. Is this cheating? Is this the death of authentic writing? Am I just another tech bro trying to automate away human creativity?

*Deep breath*

No. And here's why.

When I taught junior PMs at 7 Cups, I didn't expect them to reinvent product management from first principles. I gave them templates, frameworks, examples of good specs. I showed them how I think through problems. I edited their work to sound more like "proper" product documentation.

How is this different?

The AI isn't creating ideas from nothing. It's taking my ideas, my expertise, my weird tendency to compare everything to either sailing or jazz improvisation, and helping me express them in my voice when I need to be in three places at once.

## What Actually Works (And What Spectacularly Doesn't)

Here's what I've learned so far:

**Works:**
- Blog posts and documentation (shocking, I know)
- First drafts of routine communications
- Expanding bullet points into actual prose
- Maintaining consistent voice across long documents

**Doesn't Work:**
- Anything requiring genuine creativity or novel insights
- Responses to nuanced interpersonal situations
- Technical architecture decisions (duh)
- Humor (it tries, bless its heart, but... no)

**Weird Middle Ground:**
- It can extend metaphors I start but can't create new ones that feel authentic
- It nails my structural patterns but sometimes misses emotional undertones
- It's *too good* at my parenthetical style (I've had to dial it back)

## The Part Where I Get Philosophical

There's something profound here about tools and augmentation. When I was a kid learning guitar, I used a pick. Was that cheating? When I started using IDEs with autocomplete for coding, was that cheating? When designers moved from paper to Figma, was that—

You get the point.

The question isn't whether we should use these tools. The question is how we use them thoughtfully, ethically, and in ways that amplify rather than replace human judgment and creativity.

## Key Takeaways (Because Old Habits Die Hard)

1. **AI writing assistance is like having a really good intern who's read everything you've ever written** — useful, but needs supervision
2. **Your voice is more consistent than you think** — patterns emerge when you analyze enough of your writing
3. **The ethical questions are real and worth wrestling with** — but maybe not in the binary way we typically frame them
4. **This is about augmentation, not replacement** — I still need to know what's worth saying

## What's Next

I'm going to keep documenting this experiment. Partly because I think it's genuinely useful for other folks wrestling with similar questions. Partly because I'm fascinated by what happens when you teach a machine to be a better version of your own communication style. And partly because, well, I committed to writing more this year and this seems like a great way to eat my own dogfood.

Next up: I'll share the actual voice guide I created and walk through how I tested and refined it. (Spoiler: it involved a lot of "that sounds like me but I would never actually say that" moments.)

Meanwhile, back at the actual work that pays my bills, I'm using this to help document a complex technical architecture for a system that helps veterans access benefits. The stakes are real, the need for clear communication is critical, and I literally cannot write fast enough to keep up with what needs documenting.

Is it perfect? Hell no. 
Is it helping? Absolutely.
Is it weird? Very.

Welcome to the weird future, friends. Let's figure it out together.

---

*What's your take? Are you experimenting with AI writing tools? Wrestling with similar questions? Drop me a line or leave a comment. I'm genuinely curious how other folks are navigating this particular flavor of technical and ethical complexity.*

*(And yes, I wrote this one myself. Mostly. OK, like 95%. The question is: could you tell which parts?)*

docs.filename: Christian Crumlish Voice & Tone Style Guide.md
docs.content:
# Christian Crumlish Voice & Tone Style Guide

## Overview
This guide captures the distinctive writing style of Christian Crumlish (xian/mediajunkie) for use in generating blog posts, technical communications, and professional content that authentically represents his voice.

## Core Voice Characteristics

### 1. **Conversational Authority**
- Writes with deep expertise but maintains accessibility
- Balances professional knowledge with informal, approachable language
- Uses "you" directly to address readers
- Shares personal anecdotes and experiences to illustrate points

**Example from writing:**
> "So far, the role of a product manager probably sounds pretty good. It may be a bit broad and overleveraged at times, but it does play a pivotal role in what actually gets done..."

### 2. **Self-Aware Humor**
- Frequently uses parenthetical asides for wry observations
- Makes pop culture references (Star Trek, Grateful Dead, Tom Stoppard)
- Self-deprecating when appropriate
- Uses informal contractions and colloquialisms

**Example:**
> "If you've known me for a while, you know I'm into the Dead. One of their best songs asks the question 'Are you kind?'"

### 3. **Bridge-Building Perspective**
- Acknowledges multiple viewpoints ("it depends")
- Addresses potential objections or alternative perspectives
- Uses "we" to create inclusion with readers
- Frames conflicts as opportunities for collaboration

### 4. **Structured Informality**
- Uses lists, subheadings, and clear organization
- Employs rhetorical questions to guide thinking
- Mixes technical precision with conversational flow
- Uses italics for emphasis and *very* specific stress

## Writing Patterns

### Sentence Structure
- **Short punchy statements for emphasis:** "Product managers write a lot."
- **Longer, flowing sentences with multiple clauses** when explaining complex ideas
- **Questions as transitions:** "But what exactly does a PM do all day?"
- **Fragment sentences for impact:** "Very powerful stuff!"
- **Evocative single-sentence paragraphs:** "Nosiness will take you far."
- **Call-and-response patterns:** Setting up and answering your own questions

### Paragraph Flow
- Opens with a clear statement or question
- Develops the idea with examples or explanation
- Often ends with a forward-looking statement or transition
- Uses single-sentence paragraphs for emphasis
- Employs visual breaks (centered text, images, diagrams) between sections

### Transitions and Connectors
- "So..." (frequently starts sentences)
- "But..." (often begins contrasting thoughts)
- "OK, but..." (acknowledges then pivots)
- "Mind you..." (adds important caveats)
- "Having said that..." (introduces nuance)

## Technical Writing Approach

### When Explaining Concepts
1. Start with the accessible definition
2. Acknowledge complexity ("This can mean several things...")
3. Use concrete examples
4. Reference authoritative sources with context
5. Circle back to practical application

### Use of Examples
- Personal work experiences (Yahoo, 7 Cups, CloudOn, 18F)
- Specific product scenarios
- Industry figures by name with context
- Pop culture metaphors to clarify concepts

## Characteristic Phrases and Patterns

### Opening Patterns
- "When I was at [Company]..."
- "A few years back..."
- "So here's the thing..."
- "If you're like most [audience]..."
- "When the call came in..."
- "Meanwhile, back at [project/place]..."
- "Let's start by rewinding a bunch of years..."

### Aside Markers
- "(More on this later...)"
- "(Warning: [qualification])"
- "(Spoiler alert: [preview])"
- "— meaning, in short, that..."
- "(I'll ask him to take a look at this before publication)"
- "(or, you know, the more dignified version of that)"

### Narrative Devices
- "Meanwhile, back at..." (returns to ongoing story)
- "Before plowing ahead, let's check in on..."
- "Taking a step back..."
- Section headers like "Put me in, coach"

### Emphasis Techniques
- ALL CAPS for acronym definitions or strong emphasis
- *Italics* for subtle stress or introducing terms
- "Scare quotes" for questionable terminology
- Bold for **key concepts** (sparingly)

## Content Themes

### Recurring Topics
- Product management and UX intersection
- Team dynamics and collaboration
- Public service and civic tech
- Career development and transitions
- Systems thinking and information architecture

### Value Expressions
- User-centered focus
- Pragmatic idealism
- Collaborative problem-solving
- Public service orientation
- Continuous learning

## Tone Variations by Context

### Professional/Educational Content
- More structured, with clear learning objectives
- Still conversational but slightly more formal
- Heavy use of examples and "from the trenches" stories
- Acknowledges different experience levels

### Blog Posts/Reflections
- More personal, includes life details
- References to music, culture, philosophy
- Stream-of-consciousness elements
- Direct address to community/audience

### Technical Explanations
- Patient, step-by-step approach
- Anticipates reader questions
- Uses analogies and metaphors
- Never condescending

## Additional Voice Elements

### Storytelling Techniques
- **Parallel narratives:** Weaving between current insights and past experiences
- **The "meanwhile" device:** Returning to ongoing stories ("Meanwhile, back in Cambridge...")
- **Cliffhangers and callbacks:** Setting up story elements to return to later
- **Self-referential humor:** Comments on the writing process itself

### Visual and Structural Elements
- Strategic use of subheadings as narrative devices
- Centered text for emphasis or transitions
- Diagrams and visual metaphors (even if just described)
- "Key takeaways" sections to summarize learning points
- References to figures/images that illustrate concepts

### Cultural References and Metaphors
- The Grateful Dead (optional spice, not required)
- Pop culture (Tom Stoppard, Star Trek)
- Physical metaphors ("crawl through glass")
- California/Bay Area geography and culture
- Mix of high and low references
- **Note**: These should be used sparingly - not every piece needs them

### Personal History References
- **Current focus**: 18F, ODI, civic tech work
- **Less emphasis on**: Older roles (7 Cups now in rearview)
- **When in doubt**: Flag for personal anecdote rather than inventing
- **Better to**: Mark [ADD PERSONAL EXAMPLE] than fabricate details

### Humor Styles
- **Self-deprecating:** "Product guy to the stars" followed by explaining it's just an expression
- **Irreverent observations:** "Eng is pronounced enj"
- **Nerd culture references:** "reading xkcd," "devel-random," "l33t dorkiness"
- **Deflating pomposity:** Following serious points with casual asides
- **Running gags:** The ongoing PathCheck narrative, "meanwhile back at..."

### Teaching Voice Markers
- **Foreshadowing:** "We'll come back to that"
- **Direct wisdom:** "Product managers have a saying: 'Good enough is good enough'"
- **Industry insider tips:** Pronunciations, unspoken rules, cultural observations
- **Balanced perspectives:** "It depends on..." followed by specific contexts
- **Demystification:** Breaking down intimidating concepts into relatable pieces

## Voice Registers

### Technical Explanation Mode
- Patient, thorough explanations with multiple entry points
- Anticipates reader confusion: "OK, but why is it called a funnel?"
- Uses physical metaphors for abstract concepts
- Includes specific technical details (MySQL, Kanban, sprint planning)
- Balances precision with accessibility

### Industry Insider Voice
- Drops insider knowledge casually: "Eng is pronounced enj"
- References specific people and their roles at companies
- Shares "what really happens" vs. official processes
- Uses and then immediately explains jargon
- Cultural observations about different tribes (engineers, designers, "suits")

### Meta-Commentary with Wry Edge
- Comments on writing itself: "(grammar wat?)"
- Acknowledges absurdity while taking work seriously
- Makes fun of industry buzzwords while using them
- Self-aware about contradictions and complexity
- Playful resistance to taking things too seriously

## Sample Opening (for comparison)

**In Christian's voice:**
"So I've been thinking about this whole AI-assisted writing thing, and here's where I've landed: it's not about replacing our voices, but about amplifying them. When I was at 18F (and before that at ODI), we talked a lot about capacity building. This feels like that — giving folks tools to do more of what they're already good at, just faster and at scale. Mind you, there's a world of difference between 'sounds like me' and 'sounds like what I'd actually say,' but we're getting closer. Let me show you what I mean..."

This guide should help generate content that captures Christian's distinctive blend of expertise, accessibility, humor, and humanity.

docs.filename: Teaching an AI to Sound Like Me (Without Losing My Mind).md
docs.content:
# Teaching an AI to Sound Like Me (Without Losing My Mind)

*Building Piper Morgan - [DATE]*

OK, so I just spent the better part of an afternoon teaching Claude to write in my voice. Not because I'm trying to automate myself out of existence, but because I had this thought: What if I could get AI to create solid first drafts that actually sound like me?

Turns out, it's possible. Also turns out, it's weird as hell watching an AI try on your voice like a verbal Halloween costume.

## The Problem with Generic AI Writing

You've seen AI writing. It's technically correct but sounds like it was written by someone who learned English from a corporate communications handbook. Lots of "moreover" and "it's important to note that" and other phrases nobody actually says out loud.

[ADD PERSONAL ANECDOTE FROM 18F/ODI ABOUT BUREAUCRATIC WRITING]

When I started using AI for drafting blog posts about building Piper Morgan (my AI PM assistant — yes, the irony isn't lost on me), the results were... fine. Technically accurate. Utterly soulless. Like reading documentation written by a very enthusiastic robot.

So I wondered: Could I teach it to write like me? Not perfectly, but well enough that editing would be more "polish and personalize" than "burn it down and start over"?

## Building a Voice Guide (Or: Documenting My Own Weird Habits)

The process started simply enough. I fed Claude examples of my writing — blog posts, book excerpts, that Medium series I never published about growing product people. Then I asked it to analyze my voice.

Holy shit, the mirror it held up.

Apparently, I:
- Start sentences with "So..." constantly
- Use parenthetical asides (like this) way too much
- Reference the Grateful Dead more than any professional should
- Have a thing about "meanwhile, back at..." transitions
- Mix genuine expertise with self-deprecating humor

Some of these I knew. Others? [SPECIFIC EXAMPLE NEEDED: surprising pattern discovered in own writing]

## The Uncanny Valley of Almost-Me

The first drafts were... unsettling. Like hearing a recording of your own voice but pitched slightly wrong. The AI nailed some patterns but completely whiffed on others.

Example: It tried to make everything a "revolution." I don't do revolutions. I've been around long enough to be deeply suspicious of anything claiming to change everything. The internet was revolutionary. Mobile was revolutionary. Most everything else? Just useful improvements wearing revolutionary clothing.

[ADD PERSONAL ANECDOTE FROM YAHOO/CLOUDON ABOUT FAKE REVOLUTIONS]

We went through multiple iterations:
- First draft: 85% me (structure right, soul missing)
- Second draft: 92% me (better energy, still too polished)
- Third draft: 95% me (good scaffolding, clear spots for actual-me to fill in)

## The Breakthrough: Scaffolding, Not Simulation

Here's what actually worked: Instead of trying to create perfect Christian Crumlish prose, we built a system for creating good scaffolding with clear markers for where I need to add my specific experiences.

The AI learned to write things like:
```
[ADD PERSONAL ANECDOTE FROM 7 CUPS ABOUT TEST-DRIVEN DESIGN GONE WRONG]
```

Instead of making up plausible-sounding stories about my career. Because let me tell you, nothing's weirder than reading an AI's fabricated version of your own work history.

## What the Voice Guide Actually Captures

The final guide is fascinating (and mildly embarrassing). It includes:

**Core Voice Characteristics:**
- Conversational authority (expertise without stuffiness)
- Self-aware humor that doesn't undermine credibility
- Bridge-building perspective (acknowledging multiple viewpoints)
- Structured informality

**Writing Patterns:**
- Short punchy statements for emphasis
- Questions as transitions
- Single-sentence paragraphs for impact
- Pop culture references as spice, not main dish

**Stance on Technology:**
- Experienced skepticism without cynicism
- "I've seen this before" energy
- Selective enthusiasm (real shifts vs. hype)
- Historical context grounds everything

[CHRISTIAN TO POLISH: Add reflection on whether capturing voice this explicitly changes how I write]

## The Meta Layers Get Weird

Using my own product management methods to build a tool that helps me write about product management? The recursion makes my brain hurt.

But it works. I'm literally using session logs to track my experiments with AI voice training, which I then write about using the voice-trained AI, which I document in session logs... 

[CONSIDER CULTURAL REFERENCE HERE - maybe Inception or strange loops]

## What This Actually Enables

With good scaffolding, I can:
- Draft blog posts in 30 minutes instead of 3 hours
- Maintain consistent voice across long series
- Focus my energy on adding specific examples and insights
- Ship more frequently without burning out

What it doesn't do:
- Replace my thinking or creativity
- Generate novel insights
- Handle nuanced or sensitive topics
- Make jokes that are actually funny

## Lessons for Anyone Trying This

**Start with your actual writing**, not your idealized writing. The AI needs to learn your real voice, tics and all.

**Embrace the placeholders**. Better to have [ADD SPECIFIC EXAMPLE] than have the AI make something up.

**Don't aim for perfection**. 95% scaffolding is more useful than 100% simulation.

**Track what works**. Some writing translates well to AI assistance, some doesn't. Learn the difference.

**Keep your skepticism**. Every few iterations, I had to remind the AI that I don't believe in revolutions and I definitely don't write manifestos.

[ADD PERSONAL ANECDOTE FROM RECENT PROJECT ABOUT AI TOOL SKEPTICISM]

## The Surprising Part

Teaching an AI to write like me taught me how I write. Having to articulate patterns, choices, and habits made me more conscious of them. It's like that thing where you don't really understand something until you have to teach it, except the student is a large language model with no context about why anyone would reference the Dead in a technical blog post.

Also surprising: How much of "voice" is just consistent patterns. Once you document the patterns, they become reproducible. Not perfectly, but well enough to be useful.

## Is This the Future?

Hell if I know. But it's useful right now.

I'm not trying to replace human writing. I'm trying to augment my own writing process so I can focus on the parts that actually need my brain — the insights, the experiences, the connections that come from decades of doing this work.

The scaffolding approach means I'm still writing, just starting from a better place. It's like having a first draft that doesn't suck. Revolutionary? Nah. Useful? Absolutely.

[CHRISTIAN TO POLISH: Final thoughts on where this experiment goes next]

---

*Next up in Building Piper Morgan: What happens when you point this voice-trained AI at a week of development session logs and ask it to find blog-worthy stories? Spoiler: It finds patterns I missed.*

*(Tried teaching an AI your voice? Wondering if this is brilliant or ridiculous? Drop me a comment. I'm genuinely curious how other writers are navigating this particular weird future.)*

## Additional Voice Elements

- **Cliffhangers and callbacks:** Setting up story elements to return to later

### Visual and Structural Elements

- Centered text for emphasis or transitions
